**Iceland**[[d]](#cite_note-13) is a [Nordic](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") [island country](/wiki/Island_country "Island country") between the [Arctic Ocean](/wiki/Arctic_Ocean "Arctic Ocean") and the [North Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), located on the [Mid-Atlantic Ridge](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_Ridge "Mid-Atlantic Ridge") between [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"). It is culturally and politically linked with Europe and is the region's westernmost and most [sparsely populated country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density").[[10]](#cite_note-Statice-14) Its [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and largest city is [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), which is home to about 35% of the country's roughly 395,000 residents (excluding nearby towns/suburbs, which are separate municipalities). The official language of the country is [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"). Iceland is on a [rift](/wiki/Rift "Rift") between [tectonic plates](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"), and its geologic activity includes [geysers](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser") and frequent [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Types_of_volcanic_eruptions "Types of volcanic eruptions").[[11]](#cite_note-15)[[12]](#cite_note-16) The interior consists of a [volcanic plateau](/wiki/Volcanic_plateau "Volcanic plateau") with sand and [lava fields](/wiki/Lava_field "Lava field"), mountains and [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier"), and many [glacial rivers](/wiki/Glacial_stream "Glacial stream") flow to the sea through the [lowlands](/wiki/Upland_and_lowland "Upland and lowland"). Iceland is warmed by the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") and has a [temperate climate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate"), despite being at a latitude just south of the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle"). Its latitude and marine influence keep summers chilly, and most of its [islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Iceland "List of islands of Iceland") have a [polar climate](/wiki/Polar_climate "Polar climate").

According to the *[Landnámabók](/wiki/Landn%C3%A1mab%C3%B3k "Landnámabók")*, an ancient manuscript, the [settlement of Iceland](/wiki/Settlement_of_Iceland "Settlement of Iceland") began in 874 AD, when the [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegians "Norwegians") chieftain [Ingólfr Arnarson](/wiki/Ing%C3%B3lfr_Arnarson "Ingólfr Arnarson") became the island's first permanent settler.[[13]](#cite_note-tomasson-17) In following centuries, Norwegians and to a lesser extent other [Scandinavians](/wiki/Scandinavian_people "Scandinavian people"), immigrated to Iceland, bringing with them [thralls](/wiki/Thrall "Thrall") (i.e., slaves or serfs) of [Gaelic](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels") origin. The island was governed as an [independent commonwealth](/wiki/Icelandic_Commonwealth "Icelandic Commonwealth") under the native parliament, the [Althing](/wiki/Althing "Althing"), one of the world's oldest functioning legislative assemblies. After a [period of civil strife](/wiki/Age_of_the_Sturlungs "Age of the Sturlungs"), Iceland acceded to Norwegian rule in the 13th century. In 1397, Iceland followed Norway's integration into the [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") along with the kingdoms of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), coming under *de facto* [Danish](/wiki/Danes "Danes") rule upon its dissolution in 1523. The Danish kingdom introduced [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") by force in 1550,[[14]](#cite_note-18) and the [Treaty of Kiel](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kiel "Treaty of Kiel") formally ceded Iceland to Denmark in 1814.

[Influenced](/wiki/Influence_of_the_French_Revolution "Influence of the French Revolution") by ideals of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") after the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), Iceland's [struggle for independence](/wiki/Icelandic_independence_movement "Icelandic independence movement") took form culminating with the [Danish–Icelandic Act of Union](/wiki/Danish%E2%80%93Icelandic_Act_of_Union "Danish–Icelandic Act of Union") in 1918, with the establishment of the [Kingdom of Iceland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iceland "Kingdom of Iceland"), sharing through a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") the incumbent [monarch of Denmark](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Denmark "Monarchy of Denmark"). During the [occupation of Denmark](/wiki/Occupation_of_Denmark "Occupation of Denmark") in World War II, Iceland voted overwhelmingly to [become a republic](/wiki/1944_Icelandic_constitutional_referendum "1944 Icelandic constitutional referendum") in 1944, ending the remaining formal ties to Denmark. Although the Althing was suspended from 1799 to 1845, Iceland nevertheless has a claim to sustaining one of the world's longest-running parliaments. Until the 20th century, Iceland relied largely on [subsistence fishing](/wiki/Artisanal_fishing "Artisanal fishing") and agriculture. Industrialization of the fisheries and [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") aid after World War II brought prosperity, and Iceland became one of the world's wealthiest and most developed nations. In 1950, Iceland joined the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe").[[15]](#cite_note-19) In 1994 it became a part of the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area"), further diversifying [its economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Iceland "Economy of Iceland") into sectors such as finance, biotechnology, and [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_Iceland "Manufacturing in Iceland").

Iceland has a [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") with relatively low [taxes](/wiki/Taxation_in_Iceland "Taxation in Iceland"), compared to other [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries,[[16]](#cite_note-20) as well as the [highest trade union membership in the world](/wiki/Trade_union#Prevalence_worldwide "Trade union").[[17]](#cite_note-ILO_Density-21) It maintains a [Nordic social welfare system](/wiki/Nordic_model "Nordic model") that provides [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") and [tertiary education](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education").[[18]](#cite_note-22) [Iceland ranks highly in international comparisons](/wiki/International_rankings_of_Iceland "International rankings of Iceland") of national performance, such as quality of life, education, protection of civil liberties, government transparency, and economic freedom. It has the smallest population of any [NATO member](/wiki/Member_states_of_NATO "Member states of NATO") and is the only one with [no standing army](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_without_armed_forces "List of sovereign states without armed forces"), possessing only a lightly armed [coast guard](/wiki/Icelandic_Coast_Guard "Icelandic Coast Guard").[[19]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Norsemen_Landing_in_Iceland.jpg/250px-Norsemen_Landing_in_Iceland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norsemen_Landing_in_Iceland.jpg)

Norsemen landing in Iceland – a 19th-century depiction by [Oscar Wergeland](/wiki/Oscar_Wergeland "Oscar Wergeland")

The *Landnámabók* names [Naddodd](/wiki/Naddodd "Naddodd") ([Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse_language "Old Norse language"): *Naddoðr*) as the first [Norseman](/wiki/Norsemen "Norsemen") to reach Iceland in the ninth century, having gotten lost while sailing from Norway to the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"). He gave the island its first name of *Snæland* (English: *Snowland*). The second explorer to arrive was the Swedish [Garðar Svavarsson](/wiki/Gar%C3%B0ar_Svavarsson "Garðar Svavarsson"), who circumnavigated the island and named it *Garðarshólmur* (English: *Garðar's Isle*) after himself.[[20]](#cite_note-24)

The island's present name originated from [Flóki Vilgerðarson](/wiki/Hrafna-Fl%C3%B3ki_Vilger%C3%B0arson "Hrafna-Flóki Vilgerðarson"), the first Norseman to intentionally travel to Iceland. According to the [Sagas of Icelanders](/wiki/Sagas_of_Icelanders "Sagas of Icelanders"), Flóki coined the name after he climbed a mountain, despondent after a harsh winter in present-day [Vatnsfjörður](/wiki/Vatnsfj%C3%B6r%C3%B0ur "Vatnsfjörður"), and saw an [ice cap](/wiki/Ice_cap "Ice cap").[[21]](#cite_note-Evans-25) The notion that Iceland's settlers chose that name to discourage competing settlements is most likely a myth.[[21]](#cite_note-Evans-25)

## History

Main article: [History of Iceland](/wiki/History_of_Iceland "History of Iceland")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Icelandic history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Icelandic_history "Timeline of Icelandic history").

### 874–1262: settlement and Commonwealth

See also: [Settlement of Iceland](/wiki/Settlement_of_Iceland "Settlement of Iceland"), [Icelandic Commonwealth](/wiki/Icelandic_Commonwealth "Icelandic Commonwealth"), and [Christianisation of Iceland](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Iceland "Christianisation of Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Ingolf_by_Raadsig.jpg/250px-Ingolf_by_Raadsig.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ingolf_by_Raadsig.jpg)

[Ingólfr Arnarson](/wiki/Ing%C3%B3lfr_Arnarson "Ingólfr Arnarson") (modern Icelandic: *Ingólfur Arnarson*), the first permanent Scandinavian settler

According to both *[Landnámabók](/wiki/Landn%C3%A1mab%C3%B3k "Landnámabók")* and *[Íslendingabók](/wiki/%C3%8Dslendingab%C3%B3k "Íslendingabók")*, monks known as the [Papar](/wiki/Papar "Papar") lived in Iceland before Scandinavian settlers arrived, possibly members of a [Hiberno-Scottish mission](/wiki/Hiberno-Scottish_mission "Hiberno-Scottish mission"). An archaeological excavation has revealed the ruins of a cabin in [Hafnir](/wiki/Hafnir "Hafnir") on the [Reykjanes peninsula](/wiki/Southern_Peninsula_(Iceland) "Southern Peninsula (Iceland)"). [Carbon dating](/wiki/Carbon_dating "Carbon dating") indicates that it was abandoned sometime between 770 and 880.[[22]](#cite_note-26) In 2016, archaeologists uncovered a [longhouse](/wiki/Longhouse "Longhouse") in [Stöðvarfjörður](/wiki/St%C3%B6%C3%B0varfj%C3%B6r%C3%B0ur "Stöðvarfjörður") that may date to as early as 800.[[23]](#cite_note-27)

Swedish Viking explorer [Garðar Svavarsson](/wiki/Gar%C3%B0ar_Svavarsson "Garðar Svavarsson") was the first to circumnavigate Iceland in 870 and establish that it was an island.[[24]](#cite_note-28) He stayed during the winter and built a house in [Húsavík](/wiki/H%C3%BAsav%C3%ADk "Húsavík"). Garðar departed the following summer, but one of his men, [Náttfari](/wiki/N%C3%A1ttfari "Náttfari"), decided to stay behind with two slaves. Náttfari settled in what is now known as Náttfaravík, and he and his slaves became the first documented permanent residents of Iceland.[[25]](#cite_note-29)[[26]](#cite_note-30)

The Norwegian-Norse chieftain [Ingólfr Arnarson](/wiki/Ing%C3%B3lfr_Arnarson "Ingólfr Arnarson") built his homestead in present-day [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík") in 874. Ingólfr was followed by many other emigrant settlers, largely Scandinavians and their [thralls](/wiki/Thrall "Thrall"), many of whom were [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") or [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people").[[27]](#cite_note-31) By 930, most [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land") on the island had been claimed; the [Althing](/wiki/Althing "Althing"), a legislative and judicial assembly was initiated to regulate the [Icelandic Commonwealth](/wiki/Icelandic_Commonwealth "Icelandic Commonwealth"). The lack of arable land also served as an impetus to the [settlement of Greenland](/wiki/Norse_colonization_of_Greenland "Norse colonization of Greenland") starting in 986.[[28]](#cite_note-32) The period of these early settlements coincided with the [Medieval Warm Period](/wiki/Medieval_Warm_Period "Medieval Warm Period"), when temperatures were similar to those of the early 20th century.[[29]](#cite_note-33) At this time, about 25% of Iceland was covered with forest, compared to 1% in the present day.[[30]](#cite_note-34) [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") was adopted [by consensus](/wiki/Christianisation_of_Iceland "Christianisation of Iceland") around 999–1000, although [Norse paganism](/wiki/Norse_paganism "Norse paganism") persisted among segments of the population for some years afterward.[[31]](#cite_note-35)

### Iceland as a possession

#### Middle Ages

See also: [Age of the Sturlungs](/wiki/Age_of_the_Sturlungs "Age of the Sturlungs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Grassodenh%C3%A4user.jpg/250px-Grassodenh%C3%A4user.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grassodenh%C3%A4user.jpg)

[Turf houses](/wiki/Icelandic_turf_house "Icelandic turf house") have been constructed since Iceland was settled in the 9th century.

The [Icelandic Commonwealth](/wiki/Icelandic_Commonwealth "Icelandic Commonwealth"), established in the 10th century, faced internal strife during the [Age of the Sturlungs](/wiki/Age_of_the_Sturlungs "Age of the Sturlungs") (c. 1220–1264). This period was marked by violent conflicts among chieftains, notably the Sturlung family, leading to the weakening of the Commonwealth's political structure.[[32]](#cite_note-36) The culmination of the struggles resulted in the signing of the Old Covenant ([Gamli sáttmáli](/wiki/Old_Covenant_(Iceland) "Old Covenant (Iceland)")) in 1262–1264, bringing [Iceland under Norwegian rule](/wiki/History_of_Iceland "History of Iceland").[[33]](#cite_note-37)

Environmental challenges further impacted [medieval Icelandic society](/wiki/History_of_Iceland "History of Iceland").[[34]](#cite_note-38) Upon settlement, approximately 25–40% of Iceland was forested. However, extensive deforestation occurred as forests were cleared for timber, firewood, and to create grazing land for livestock. This led to significant soil erosion and a decline in arable land, exacerbating the difficulties of sustaining agriculture in Iceland's harsh climate.[[35]](#cite_note-39) Agriculture during this period was predominantly pastoral, focusing on livestock such as sheep, cattle, and horses. While early settlers cultivated barley, the cooling climate from the 12th century onwards made grain cultivation increasingly difficult. The [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age") began around 1300 bringing colder and more unpredictable weather, further shortening growing seasons and making farming more challenging.[[36]](#cite_note-40)[[37]](#cite_note-41)

The [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") reached Iceland in 1402–1404 and again in 1494–1495, with devastating effects.[[38]](#cite_note-42) The first outbreak is estimated to have killed 50–60% of the population, while the second resulted in a 30–50% mortality rate. These pandemics significantly reduced the population, leading to social and economic disruptions.[[39]](#cite_note-43)

In 1380, [Olaf II of Denmark](/wiki/Olaf_II_of_Denmark "Olaf II of Denmark") became King of Norway on his father's death. Denmark and Norway were thus united in personal union and ruled from Denmark; this was further solidified with the formation of the [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") in 1397. Through it Iceland, as a Norwegian possession, came to be ruled by Denmark.

#### Reformation and the Early Modern period

See also: [Icelandic Reformation](/wiki/Icelandic_Reformation "Icelandic Reformation"), [Danish trade monopoly in Iceland](/wiki/Danish_trade_monopoly_in_Iceland "Danish trade monopoly in Iceland"), and [Móðuharðindin](/wiki/M%C3%B3%C3%B0uhar%C3%B0indin "Móðuharðindin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Bolungarvik_02.jpg/250px-Bolungarvik_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bolungarvik_02.jpg)

*Ósvör*, a replica of an old fishing outpost outside [Bolungarvík](/wiki/Bolungarv%C3%ADk "Bolungarvík")

Around the middle of the 16th century, as part of the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), King [Christian III of Denmark](/wiki/Christian_III_of_Denmark "Christian III of Denmark") began to impose [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") on all his subjects. [Jón Arason](/wiki/J%C3%B3n_Arason "Jón Arason"), the last Icelandic Catholic bishop, and two of his sons were beheaded in 1550. Iceland then became officially Lutheran, and Lutheranism has since remained the dominant religion.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Gerardi_Mercatoris_Atlas%2C_sive%2C_Cosmographicae_Meditationes_de_Fabrica_Mundi_et_Fabricati_Figura_%2824696368309%29.jpg/250px-Gerardi_Mercatoris_Atlas%2C_sive%2C_Cosmographicae_Meditationes_de_Fabrica_Mundi_et_Fabricati_Figura_%2824696368309%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gerardi_Mercatoris_Atlas,_sive,_Cosmographicae_Meditationes_de_Fabrica_Mundi_et_Fabricati_Figura_(24696368309).jpg)

A map of Iceland published in the early 17th century by [Gerardus Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator")

In the 17th and 18th centuries, Denmark imposed [harsh trade restrictions](/wiki/Danish%E2%80%93Icelandic_Trade_Monopoly "Danish–Icelandic Trade Monopoly") on Iceland. Natural disasters, including volcanic eruptions and disease, contributed to a decreasing population. In the summer of 1627, [Barbary Pirates](/wiki/Barbary_Pirates "Barbary Pirates") committed the events known locally as the [Turkish Abductions](/wiki/Turkish_Abductions "Turkish Abductions"), in which hundreds of residents were taken into slavery in North Africa and dozens killed; this was the only invasion in Icelandic history to have casualties.[[40]](#cite_note-44)[[41]](#cite_note-45) The [1707–08 Iceland smallpox epidemic](/wiki/1707%E2%80%9308_Iceland_smallpox_epidemic "1707–08 Iceland smallpox epidemic") is estimated to have killed a quarter to a third of the population.[[42]](#cite_note-46)[[43]](#cite_note-47) In 1783 the [Laki](/wiki/Laki "Laki") volcano erupted, with devastating effects.[[44]](#cite_note-48) In the years following the eruption, known as the [Mist Hardships](/wiki/Mist_Hardships "Mist Hardships") ([Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"): *Móðuharðindin*), over half of all livestock in the country died. Around a quarter of the population starved to death in the ensuing [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine").[[45]](#cite_note-49)

#### 1814–1918: independence movement

See also: [Icelandic independence movement](/wiki/Icelandic_independence_movement "Icelandic independence movement") and [Fjölnir (journal)](/wiki/Fj%C3%B6lnir_(journal) "Fjölnir (journal)")

After the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") in 1814, Denmark-Norway broke up into two separate kingdoms via the [Treaty of Kiel](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kiel "Treaty of Kiel"), but Iceland remained a Danish dependency. Throughout the 19th century, the country's climate continued to grow colder, resulting in mass emigration to the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), particularly to the region of [Gimli, Manitoba](/wiki/Gimli,_Manitoba "Gimli, Manitoba") in Canada, which was sometimes referred to as [New Iceland](/wiki/New_Iceland "New Iceland"). About 15,000 people emigrated out of a total population of 70,000.[[46]](#cite_note-50)

A national consciousness arose in the first half of the 19th century, inspired by [romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") and [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") ideas from mainland Europe. An Icelandic independence movement took shape in the 1850s under the leadership of [Jón Sigurðsson](/wiki/J%C3%B3n_Sigur%C3%B0sson "Jón Sigurðsson"), based on the burgeoning Icelandic nationalism inspired by the *[Fjölnismenn](/wiki/Fj%C3%B6lnismenn "Fjölnismenn")* and other Danish-educated Icelandic intellectuals. In 1874, poet and clergyman [Matthías Jochumsson](/wiki/Matth%C3%ADas_Jochumsson "Matthías Jochumsson") wrote the lyrics of "[Lofsöngur](/wiki/Lofs%C3%B6ngur "Lofsöngur")," which became the Icelandic [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem"). Composer [Sveinbjörn Sveinbjörnsson](/wiki/Sveinbj%C3%B6rn_Sveinbj%C3%B6rnsson "Sveinbjörn Sveinbjörnsson") wrote the music.

In 1874, Denmark granted Iceland a constitution and limited home rule. That was expanded in 1904, and [Hannes Hafstein](/wiki/Hannes_Hafstein "Hannes Hafstein") served as the first [Minister for Iceland](/wiki/Minister_for_Iceland "Minister for Iceland") in the Danish cabinet.

### 1918–1944: independence and the Kingdom of Iceland

See also: [Kingdom of Iceland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iceland "Kingdom of Iceland"), [Invasion of Iceland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Iceland "Invasion of Iceland"), and [Iceland in World War II](/wiki/Iceland_in_World_War_II "Iceland in World War II")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/HMS_Berwick_%2865%29.jpg/250px-HMS_Berwick_%2865%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Berwick_(65).jpg)

[HMS *Berwick*](/wiki/HMS_Berwick_(65) "HMS Berwick (65)") led the British [invasion of Iceland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Iceland "Invasion of Iceland").

The [Danish–Icelandic Act of Union](/wiki/Danish%E2%80%93Icelandic_Act_of_Union "Danish–Icelandic Act of Union"), an agreement with Denmark signed on 1 December 1918 and valid for 25 years, recognized Iceland as a fully sovereign and independent state in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") with Denmark. The Government of Iceland established an embassy in Copenhagen and requested that Denmark carry out on its behalf certain defence and foreign affairs matters, subject to consultation with the Althing. Danish embassies around the world displayed two coats of arms and two flags: those of the Kingdom of Denmark and those of the [Kingdom of Iceland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iceland "Kingdom of Iceland"). Iceland's legal position became comparable to those of countries belonging to the Commonwealth of Nations, such as Canada, whose sovereign is King [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III").

During World War II, Iceland joined Denmark in asserting neutrality. After the [German occupation of Denmark](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Denmark "German occupation of Denmark") on 9 April 1940, the Althing replaced the King with a regent and declared that the Icelandic government would take control of its own defence and foreign affairs.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-51) A month later, British armed forces conducted [Operation Fork](/wiki/Operation_Fork "Operation Fork"), the invasion and occupation of the country, violating Icelandic [neutrality](/wiki/Neutrality_(international_relations) "Neutrality (international relations)").[[48]](#cite_note-52) In 1941, the Government of Iceland, friendly to Britain, invited the then-neutral United States to take over its defence so that Britain could use its troops elsewhere.[[47]](#cite_note-:0-51)

### 1944–present: Republic of Iceland

See also: [Icelandic constitutional referendum, 1944](/wiki/Icelandic_constitutional_referendum,_1944 "Icelandic constitutional referendum, 1944"); [Iceland in the Cold War](/wiki/Iceland_in_the_Cold_War "Iceland in the Cold War"); and [Cod Wars](/wiki/Cod_Wars "Cod Wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Scylla-Odinn.jpg/250px-Scylla-Odinn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scylla-Odinn.jpg)

A British warship, [HMS *Scylla*](/wiki/HMS_Scylla_(F71) "HMS Scylla (F71)") (right), collides with Icelandic coast guard vessel [ICGV *Óðinn*](/wiki/ICGV_%C3%93%C3%B0inn "ICGV Óðinn") in the Atlantic Ocean during the [Third Cod War](/wiki/Cod_Wars "Cod Wars").

On 31 December 1943, the [Danish–Icelandic Act of Union](/wiki/Danish%E2%80%93Icelandic_Act_of_Union "Danish–Icelandic Act of Union") expired after 25 years. Beginning on 20 May 1944, Icelanders voted in a four-day plebiscite on whether to terminate the personal union with Denmark, abolish the monarchy, and establish a republic. The vote was 97% to end the union, and 95% in favor of the new republican constitution.[[49]](#cite_note-53) Iceland formally became a republic on 17 June 1944, with [Sveinn Björnsson](/wiki/Sveinn_Bj%C3%B6rnsson "Sveinn Björnsson") as its first president.

In 1946, the U.S. Defense Force left Iceland. The nation formally became a member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") on 30 March 1949, amid [domestic controversy and riots](/wiki/1949_anti-NATO_riot_in_Iceland "1949 anti-NATO riot in Iceland"). On 5 May 1951, a defense agreement was signed with the United States. American troops returned to Iceland as the [Iceland Defence Force](/wiki/Iceland_Defence_Force "Iceland Defence Force") and remained throughout the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). The U.S. withdrew the last of its forces on 30 September 2006.

Iceland prospered during the Second World War. The immediate post-war period was followed by substantial [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth"), driven by the industrialization of the fishing industry and the U.S. [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") program, through which Icelanders received the most aid per capita of any European country (at US$209, with war-ravaged [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") a distant second at US$109).[[50]](#cite_note-54)[[51]](#cite_note-55) [Vigdís Finnbogadóttir](/wiki/Vigd%C3%ADs_Finnbogad%C3%B3ttir "Vigdís Finnbogadóttir") assumed Iceland's presidency on 1 August 1980, making her the first elected female head of state in the world.[[52]](#cite_note-56)

The 1970s were marked by the [Cod Wars](/wiki/Cod_Wars "Cod Wars"), which were several disputes with the United Kingdom over Iceland's extension of its fishing limits to 200 nmi (370 km) offshore. Iceland hosted a [summit in Reykjavík in 1986](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_Summit "Reykjavík Summit") between United States President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") and Soviet Premier [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev"), during which they took significant steps towards [nuclear disarmament](/wiki/Nuclear_disarmament "Nuclear disarmament"). A few years later, Iceland became the first country to recognize the independence of [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") as they [broke away](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") from the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"). Throughout the 1990s, the country expanded its international role and developed a foreign policy oriented towards humanitarian and peacekeeping causes. To that end, Iceland provided aid and expertise to various NATO-led interventions in [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War"), and [Iraq](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War").[[53]](#cite_note-57)

Iceland joined the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") in 1994, after which the economy was greatly diversified and liberalised. International economic relations increased further after 2001 when Iceland's newly deregulated banks began to raise great amounts of [external debt](/wiki/External_debt "External debt"), contributing to a 32 percent increase in Iceland's [gross national income](/wiki/Gross_national_income "Gross national income") between 2002 and 2007.[[54]](#cite_note-jackson-58)[[55]](#cite_note-59)

#### Economic boom and crisis

Further information: [2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis") and [2009 Icelandic financial crisis protests](/wiki/2009_Icelandic_financial_crisis_protests "2009 Icelandic financial crisis protests")

In 2003–2007, after the privatization of the banking sector under the government of [Davíð Oddsson](/wiki/Dav%C3%AD%C3%B0_Oddsson "Davíð Oddsson"), Iceland moved towards having an economy based on international investment banking and financial services.[[56]](#cite_note-Lewis-60) It was quickly becoming one of the most prosperous countries in the world, but was hit hard by a [major financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis").[[56]](#cite_note-Lewis-60) The crisis resulted in the greatest migration from Iceland since 1887, with a net emigration of 5,000 people in 2009.[[57]](#cite_note-nordregio.se-61)

#### Since 2012

Iceland's economy stabilized under the government of [Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir](/wiki/J%C3%B3hanna_Sigur%C3%B0ard%C3%B3ttir "Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir") growing by 1.6% in 2012.[[58]](#cite_note-62) The centre-right [Independence Party](/wiki/Independence_Party_(Iceland) "Independence Party (Iceland)") was returned to power in coalition with the [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(Iceland) "Progressive Party (Iceland)") in the 2013 election.[[59]](#cite_note-63) In the following years, Iceland benefited from a surge in tourism as the country became a popular holiday destination. In 2016, Prime Minister [Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson](/wiki/Sigmundur_Dav%C3%AD%C3%B0_Gunnlaugsson "Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson") resigned after being implicated in the [Panama Papers](/wiki/Panama_Papers "Panama Papers") scandal.[[60]](#cite_note-64) Early elections in 2016 resulted in a right-wing coalition government of the [Independence Party](/wiki/Independence_Party_(Iceland) "Independence Party (Iceland)"), [Viðreisn](/wiki/Vi%C3%B0reisn "Viðreisn") and [Bright Future](/wiki/Bright_Future_(Iceland) "Bright Future (Iceland)").[[61]](#cite_note-65) This government fell when Bright Future quit the coalition due to a scandal involving then-Prime Minister [Bjarni Benediktsson](/wiki/Bjarni_Benediktsson_(born_1970) "Bjarni Benediktsson (born 1970)")'s father's letter of support for a convicted child sex offender.[[62]](#cite_note-66) [Snap elections in October 2017](/wiki/2017_Icelandic_parliamentary_election "2017 Icelandic parliamentary election") brought to power a new coalition consisting of the Independence Party, the Progressive Party, and the [Left-Green Movement](/wiki/Left-Green_Movement "Left-Green Movement"), headed by [Katrín Jakobsdóttir](/wiki/Katr%C3%ADn_Jakobsd%C3%B3ttir "Katrín Jakobsdóttir").[[63]](#cite_note-67)

After the 2021 parliamentary [election](/wiki/2021_Icelandic_parliamentary_election "2021 Icelandic parliamentary election"), the new government was, just like the previous government, a tri-party coalition of the Independence Party, the Progressive Party, and the Left-Green Movement, headed by Prime Minister Katrín Jakobsdóttir.[[64]](#cite_note-68) In April 2024, Bjarni Benediktsson of the Independence party succeeded Katrín Jakobsdóttir as prime minister.[[65]](#cite_note-69) In November 2024, centre-left [Social Democratic Alliance](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Alliance "Social Democratic Alliance") became the biggest party in a [snap election](/wiki/2024_Icelandic_parliamentary_election "2024 Icelandic parliamentary election"), meaning Social Democratic [Kristrun Frostadottir](/wiki/Kristr%C3%BAn_Frostad%C3%B3ttir "Kristrún Frostadóttir") became the next Prime Minister of Iceland.[[66]](#cite_note-70)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Iceland](/wiki/Geography_of_Iceland "Geography of Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Map_of_Iceland.svg/500px-Map_of_Iceland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Iceland.svg)

General topographic map

Iceland is at the juncture of the Arctic Ocean and the North Atlantic Ocean. The main island is entirely south of the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle"), which passes through the small Icelandic island of [Grímsey](/wiki/Gr%C3%ADmsey "Grímsey") off the main island's northern coast. The country lies between latitudes [63](/wiki/Circles_of_latitude_between_the_60th_parallel_north_and_the_65th_parallel_north#63rd_parallel_north "Circles of latitude between the 60th parallel north and the 65th parallel north") and [68°N](/wiki/Circles_of_latitude_between_the_65th_parallel_north_and_the_70th_parallel_north#68th_parallel_north "Circles of latitude between the 65th parallel north and the 70th parallel north"), and longitudes [25](/wiki/25th_meridian_west "25th meridian west") and [13°W](/wiki/13th_meridian_west "13th meridian west").

Iceland is closer to [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") than to mainland North America, although it is closest to [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), an island of North America. Iceland is generally included in Europe for geographical, historical, political, cultural, linguistic, and practical reasons.[[67]](#cite_note-Ingebritsen2000-71)[[68]](#cite_note-LehtiSmith2003-72)[[69]](#cite_note-Miles2005-73)[[70]](#cite_note-Dhondt2011-74) Geologically, the island includes parts of both continental plates. The closest bodies of land in Europe are the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands") (420 km; 225 nmi); [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") Island (570 km; 310 nmi); [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland") and the [Outer Hebrides](/wiki/Outer_Hebrides "Outer Hebrides"), both about 740 km (400 nmi); and the Scottish mainland and [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney"), both about 750 km (405 nmi). The nearest part of Continental Europe is mainland Norway, about 970 km (525 nmi) away, while mainland North America is 2,070 km (1,120 nmi) away, at the northern tip of [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Icelandic_landscapes.jpg/250px-Icelandic_landscapes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Icelandic_landscapes.jpg)

Three typical Icelandic landscapes

Iceland is the [18th-largest island in the world](/wiki/List_of_islands_by_area "List of islands by area"), and the second-largest island in Europe, smaller than Great Britain and bigger than Ireland.[[71]](#cite_note-75) The main island covers 101,826 km2 (39,315 sq mi), but the entire country is 103,000 km2 (40,000 sq mi) in size, of which 62.7% is [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra"). Iceland contains about 30 minor islands, including the lightly populated Grímsey and the [Vestmannaeyjar](/wiki/Vestmannaeyjar "Vestmannaeyjar") archipelago. Lakes and glaciers cover 14.3% of its surface; only 23% is vegetated.[[72]](#cite_note-NLS-76) The largest lakes are [Þórisvatn](/wiki/%C3%9E%C3%B3risvatn "Þórisvatn") reservoir: 83–88 km2 (32–34 sq mi) and [Þingvallavatn](/wiki/%C3%9Eingvallavatn "Þingvallavatn"): 82 km2 (32 sq mi); other important lakes include [Lagarfljót](/wiki/Lagarflj%C3%B3t "Lagarfljót") and [Mývatn](/wiki/M%C3%BDvatn "Mývatn"). [Jökulsárlón](/wiki/J%C3%B6kuls%C3%A1rl%C3%B3n "Jökulsárlón") is the deepest lake, at 248 m (814 ft).[[73]](#cite_note-77) Geologically, Iceland is a part of the [Mid-Atlantic Ridge](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_Ridge "Mid-Atlantic Ridge"), a ridge along which the [oceanic crust](/wiki/Oceanic_crust "Oceanic crust") spreads and forms new crust. This part of the mid-ocean ridge is located above a [mantle plume](/wiki/Mantle_plume "Mantle plume"), causing Iceland to be [subaerial](/wiki/Subaerial "Subaerial") (above the surface of the sea). The ridge marks the boundary between the [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate") and the [North American Plate](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate"), and Iceland was created by [rifting](/wiki/Rift "Rift") and [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(geology) "Accretion (geology)") through volcanism along the ridge.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

Many [fjords](/wiki/Fjord "Fjord") punctuate Iceland's 4,970-kilometre-long (3,090 mi) coastline, which is also where most settlements are situated. The island's interior, the [Highlands of Iceland](/wiki/Highlands_of_Iceland "Highlands of Iceland"), is a cold and uninhabitable combination of sand, mountains, and [lava fields](/wiki/Lava_field "Lava field"). The major towns are the capital city of [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), along with its outlying towns of [Kópavogur](/wiki/K%C3%B3pavogur "Kópavogur"), [Hafnarfjörður](/wiki/Hafnarfj%C3%B6r%C3%B0ur "Hafnarfjörður"), and [Garðabær](/wiki/Gar%C3%B0ab%C3%A6r "Garðabær"), nearby [Reykjanesbær](/wiki/Reykjanesb%C3%A6r "Reykjanesbær") where the international airport is located, and the town of [Akureyri](/wiki/Akureyri "Akureyri") in northern Iceland. The island of Grímsey on the Arctic Circle contains the northernmost habitation of Iceland, whereas [Kolbeinsey](/wiki/Kolbeinsey "Kolbeinsey") contains the northernmost point of Iceland.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79) Iceland has [three national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Iceland "List of national parks of Iceland"): [Vatnajökull National Park](/wiki/Vatnaj%C3%B6kull_National_Park "Vatnajökull National Park"), [Snæfellsjökull National Park](/wiki/Sn%C3%A6fellsj%C3%B6kull "Snæfellsjökull"), and [Þingvellir National Park](/wiki/%C3%9Eingvellir "Þingvellir").[[76]](#cite_note-80) The country is considered a "strong performer" in environmental protection.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

### Geology

Main article: [Geology of Iceland](/wiki/Geology_of_Iceland "Geology of Iceland")

Further information: [Iceland hotspot](/wiki/Iceland_hotspot "Iceland hotspot")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Erupting_geysir.jpg/250px-Erupting_geysir.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erupting_geysir.jpg)

[Geysir](/wiki/Geysir "Geysir") which is in [Haukadalur](/wiki/Haukadalur_(Bl%C3%A1sk%C3%B3gabygg%C3%B0) "Haukadalur (Bláskógabyggð)") valley, is shown erupting. It is the oldest known [geyser](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser") in the world.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Iceland_-_2017-02-22_-_Gullfoss_-_3677.jpg/250px-Iceland_-_2017-02-22_-_Gullfoss_-_3677.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iceland_-_2017-02-22_-_Gullfoss_-_3677.jpg)

[Gullfoss](/wiki/Gullfoss "Gullfoss"), an iconic waterfall of Iceland

Iceland is the only large [insular landmass](/wiki/Island "Island") in the world which is located on a [mid-ocean ridge](/wiki/Mid-ocean_ridge "Mid-ocean ridge") above sea level. It is probably the only place in the world where the effects of two [major tectonic plates](/wiki/List_of_tectonic_plates#Major_plates "List of tectonic plates") drifting apart can easily be observed above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"). The island sits directly on the [Mid-Atlantic Ridge](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_Ridge "Mid-Atlantic Ridge"), where the [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate") and the [North American Plate](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate") are moving away from each other.[[78]](#cite_note-82)

A geologically young land at 16 to 18 million years old, Iceland is the surface expression of the [Iceland Plateau](/wiki/Iceland_Plateau "Iceland Plateau"), a [large igneous province](/wiki/Large_igneous_province "Large igneous province") forming as a result of volcanism from the [Iceland hotspot](/wiki/Iceland_hotspot "Iceland hotspot") and along the [Mid-Atlantic Ridge](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_Ridge "Mid-Atlantic Ridge"), the latter of which runs right through it.[[79]](#cite_note-83)[[80]](#cite_note-84) This means that the island is highly geologically active with many volcanoes including [Hekla](/wiki/Hekla "Hekla"), [Eldgjá](/wiki/Eldgj%C3%A1 "Eldgjá"), [Herðubreið](/wiki/Her%C3%B0ubrei%C3%B0 "Herðubreið"), and [Eldfell](/wiki/Eldfell "Eldfell").[[81]](#cite_note-85) The volcanic eruption of [Laki](/wiki/Laki "Laki") in 1783–1784 caused a famine that killed nearly a quarter of the island's population.[[82]](#cite_note-86) In addition, the eruption caused dust clouds and haze to appear over most of Europe and parts of Africa and Asia for several months afterwards, and affected climates in other areas.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

Iceland has many [geysers](/wiki/Geyser "Geyser"), including [Geysir](/wiki/Geysir "Geysir"), from which the English word is derived, and the famous [Strokkur](/wiki/Strokkur "Strokkur"), which erupts every 8–10 minutes. After a phase of inactivity, Geysir started erupting again after [a series of earthquakes in 2000](/wiki/2000_Iceland_earthquakes "2000 Iceland earthquakes"). Geysir has since grown quieter and does not erupt often.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

With the widespread availability of [geothermal power](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power") and the harnessing of many rivers and waterfalls for [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity"), most residents have access to inexpensive hot water, heating, and electricity. The island is composed primarily of [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt"), a low-[silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica") lava associated with [effusive volcanism](/wiki/Effusive_eruption "Effusive eruption"), as has also occurred in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"). Iceland, however, has a variety of volcanic types (composite and fissure), many producing more evolved lavas such as [rhyolite](/wiki/Rhyolite "Rhyolite") and [andesite](/wiki/Andesite "Andesite"). Iceland has hundreds of volcanoes with about 30 active volcanic systems.[[85]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Eyjafjallajokull-April-17.JPG/250px-Eyjafjallajokull-April-17.JPG)](/wiki/File:Eyjafjallajokull-April-17.JPG)

The [eruption of Eyjafjallajökull](/wiki/2010_eruptions_of_Eyjafjallaj%C3%B6kull "2010 eruptions of Eyjafjallajökull")

[Surtsey](/wiki/Surtsey "Surtsey"), one of the youngest islands in the world, is part of Iceland. Named after [Surtr](/wiki/Surtr "Surtr"), it rose above the ocean in a series of volcanic eruptions between 8 November 1963 and 5 June 1968.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79) Only scientists researching the growth of new life are allowed to visit the island.[[86]](#cite_note-90) The country has approximately [30 active volcanic systems](/wiki/Volcanism_of_Iceland "Volcanism of Iceland"). Within each are volcano-tectonic fissure systems, and many, but not all of them, also have at least one [central volcano](/wiki/Central_volcano "Central volcano") (mostly in the form of a [stratovolcano](/wiki/Stratovolcano "Stratovolcano"), sometimes of a [shield volcano](/wiki/Shield_volcano "Shield volcano") with a [magma chamber](/wiki/Magma_chamber "Magma chamber") underneath). Several classifications of the systems exist, for example there is one of 30 systems,[[87]](#cite_note-Andrews2008-91): 10 and one of 34 systems, with the later currently being used in Iceland itself.[[88]](#cite_note-EIV-92)

### Climate

Main articles: [Climate of Iceland](/wiki/Climate_of_Iceland "Climate of Iceland") and [Tornadoes in Iceland](/wiki/Tornadoes_in_Iceland "Tornadoes in Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Iceland_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Iceland_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Iceland_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") types of Iceland

The climate of Iceland's coast is [subarctic](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate"). The warm [North Atlantic Current](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Current "North Atlantic Current") ensures generally higher annual temperatures than in most places of similar latitude in the world. Regions in the world with similar climates include the [Aleutian Islands](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands "Aleutian Islands"), the [Alaska Peninsula](/wiki/Alaska_Peninsula "Alaska Peninsula"), and [Tierra del Fuego](/wiki/Tierra_del_Fuego "Tierra del Fuego"), although these regions are closer to the equator. Despite its proximity to the Arctic, the island's coasts remain ice-free through the winter. Ice incursions are rare, with the last having occurred on the north coast in 1969.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

The climate varies between different parts of the island. Generally speaking, the south coast is warmer, wetter, and windier than the north. The Central Highlands are the coldest part of the country. Low-lying inland areas in the north are the aridest. Snowfall in winter is more common in the north than in the south. The highest air temperature recorded was 30.5 °C (86.9 °F) on 22 June 1939 at Teigarhorn on the southeastern coast. The lowest was −38 °C (−36.4 °F) on 22 January 1918 at Grímsstaðir and Möðrudalur in the northeastern hinterland. The temperature records for Reykjavík are 26.2 °C (79.2 °F) on 30 July 2008, and −24.5 °C (−12.1 °F) on 21 January 1918.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Due to [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"), Iceland is experiencing faster [glacial retreat](/wiki/Glacial_retreat "Glacial retreat"), changing vegetation patterns, and shifting marine ecosystems.[[90]](#cite_note-94) Iceland's retreating glaciers have global and local consequences. Melting of Iceland's glaciers could raise sea levels by one centimeter (0.39 in),[[91]](#cite_note-:22-95) which could lead to erosion and flooding worldwide.[[92]](#cite_note-96) In late 2025, Iceland elevated the potential [collapse](/wiki/Abrupt_climate_change "Abrupt climate change") of the
[Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation](/wiki/Atlantic_Meridional_Overturning_Circulation "Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation") (AMOC) to a national security risk, recognizing severe climate impacts like extreme European winters, food/energy insecurity, and economic disruption as major threats.[[93]](#cite_note-97)

### Wildlife

Main article: [Wildlife of Iceland](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Iceland "Wildlife of Iceland")

The entire country is in a single [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"), the [Iceland boreal birch forests and alpine tundra](/wiki/Iceland_boreal_birch_forests_and_alpine_tundra "Iceland boreal birch forests and alpine tundra"). Some areas are covered by [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier").

#### Plants

[Phytogeographically](/wiki/Phytogeographically "Phytogeographically"), Iceland belongs to the Arctic province of the [Circumboreal Region](/wiki/Circumboreal_Region "Circumboreal Region") within the [Boreal Kingdom](/wiki/Boreal_Kingdom "Boreal Kingdom"). Vegetation is dominated by grasslands, which are regularly grazed by livestock. The most common native tree is the northern birch (*[Betula pubescens](/wiki/Betula_pubescens "Betula pubescens")*), which historically formed extensive forests across Iceland, along with aspens (*[Populus tremula](/wiki/Populus_tremula "Populus tremula")*), rowans (*[Sorbus aucuparia](/wiki/Sorbus_aucuparia "Sorbus aucuparia")*), the only native [gymnosperm](/wiki/Gymnosperm "Gymnosperm"), common juniper (*[Juniperus communis](/wiki/Juniperus_communis "Juniperus communis")*),[[94]](#cite_note-98) and other smaller trees, mainly willows (Salix spp.).

When the island was first settled, it was extensively forested, with around 30% of the land covered in trees. In the late 12th century, [Ari the Wise](/wiki/Ari_the_Wise "Ari the Wise") described it in the [Íslendingabók](/wiki/%C3%8Dslendingab%C3%B3k "Íslendingabók") as "forested from mountain to sea shore".[[95]](#cite_note-Dirt-99) Permanent human settlement greatly disturbed the isolated ecosystem of thin, volcanic soils and limited [species diversity](/wiki/Species_richness "Species richness"). The forests were heavily exploited over the centuries for firewood and timber.[[96]](#cite_note-wildlife-100) [Deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), climatic deterioration during the [Little Ice Age](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age"), and overgrazing by sheep imported by settlers caused a loss of critical topsoil due to [erosion](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"). Today, many farms have been abandoned. Three-quarters of Iceland's 100,000 square kilometres (39,000 mi2) is affected by soil erosion; 18,000 km2 (6,900 sq mi) is affected to a degree serious enough to make the land useless.[[95]](#cite_note-Dirt-99) Only a few small birch stands now exist in isolated reserves. The Icelandic Forest Service and other forestry groups promote large-scale reforestation in the country. Due to the reforestation efforts, the forest cover of Iceland has increased sixfold since the 1990s. That helps to offset carbon emissions, prevent sandstorms, and increase the productivity of farms.[[97]](#cite_note-101) The planting of new forests has increased the number of trees, but the result does not compare to the original forests. Some of the planted forests include [introduced species](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species").[[96]](#cite_note-wildlife-100) The tallest tree in Iceland is a [sitka spruce](/wiki/Sitka_spruce "Sitka spruce") planted in 1949 in [Kirkjubæjarklaustur](/wiki/Kirkjub%C3%A6jarklaustur "Kirkjubæjarklaustur"); it was measured at 25.2 m (83 ft) in 2013.[[98]](#cite_note-102) Algae including *[Chondrus crispus](/wiki/Chondrus_crispus "Chondrus crispus")*, *[Phyllphora truncata](/w/index.php?title=Phyllphora_truncata&action=edit&redlink=1 "Phyllphora truncata (page does not exist)")*, *Phyllophora crispa*, and others have been recorded from Iceland.[[99]](#cite_note-103)

#### Animals

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Alopex_lagopus_IMG_9019.JPG/250px-Alopex_lagopus_IMG_9019.JPG)](/wiki/File:Alopex_lagopus_IMG_9019.JPG)

The [Arctic fox](/wiki/Arctic_fox "Arctic fox") was the only land mammal in Iceland before the arrival of humans.

The only native land mammal when humans arrived was the [Arctic fox](/wiki/Arctic_fox "Arctic fox"),[[96]](#cite_note-wildlife-100) which came to the island at the end of the ice age, walking over the frozen sea. On rare occasions, bats have been carried to the island with the winds, but they are not able to breed there. No native or free-living reptiles or amphibians are on the island.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

The animals of Iceland include the [Icelandic sheep](/wiki/Icelandic_sheep "Icelandic sheep"), [cattle](/wiki/Icelandic_cattle "Icelandic cattle"), [chickens](/wiki/Icelandic_chicken "Icelandic chicken"), [goats](/wiki/Icelandic_goat "Icelandic goat"), the sturdy [Icelandic horse](/wiki/Icelandic_horse "Icelandic horse"), and the [Icelandic Sheepdog](/wiki/Icelandic_Sheepdog "Icelandic Sheepdog"), all descendants of animals imported by Europeans. Wild mammals include the Arctic fox, [mink](/wiki/Mink "Mink"), mice, rats, rabbits, and [reindeer](/wiki/Reindeer "Reindeer"). [Polar bears](/wiki/Polar_bears "Polar bears") occasionally visit the island, travelling from [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") on icebergs, but no Icelandic populations exist.[[101]](#cite_note-105) In June 2008, two polar bears arrived in the same month.[[102]](#cite_note-106) [Marine mammals](/wiki/Marine_mammals "Marine mammals") include the [grey seal](/wiki/Grey_seal "Grey seal") (*Halichoerus grypus*) and [harbour seal](/wiki/Harbour_seal "Harbour seal") (*Phoca vitulina*). Many species of fish live in the ocean waters surrounding Iceland, and the [fishing industry](/wiki/Fishing_industry "Fishing industry") is a major part of Iceland's economy, accounting for roughly half of the country's total exports. Birds, especially seabirds, are an important part of Iceland's animal life. [Atlantic puffins](/wiki/Atlantic_puffin "Atlantic puffin"), [skuas](/wiki/Skua "Skua"), and [black-legged kittiwakes](/wiki/Black-legged_kittiwake "Black-legged kittiwake") nest on its sea cliffs.[[103]](#cite_note-107)

[Commercial whaling](/wiki/Commercial_whaling "Commercial whaling") is practised intermittently[[104]](#cite_note-108)[[105]](#cite_note-109) along with scientific whale hunts.[[106]](#cite_note-110) Whale watching has become an important part of Iceland's economy since 1997.[[107]](#cite_note-111) Around 1,300 species of insects are known in Iceland. This is low compared with other countries (over one million species have been described worldwide). Iceland was essentially free of [mosquitoes](/wiki/Mosquitoes "Mosquitoes") until October 2025.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

## Politics

Main article: [Politics of Iceland](/wiki/Politics_of_Iceland "Politics of Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Politics_Of_Iceland.svg/960px-Politics_Of_Iceland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Politics_Of_Iceland.svg)

The political system of Iceland

Iceland is a [parliamentary](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), whereby the [president](/wiki/President_of_Iceland "President of Iceland") is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), while the [prime minister of Iceland](/wiki/Prime_minister_of_Iceland "Prime minister of Iceland") serves as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") in a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). Members of the [Icelandic parliament](/wiki/Althing "Althing") are voted in by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), by [constituency](/wiki/Constituency "Constituency").

After the [2024 parliamentary elections](/wiki/2024_Icelandic_parliamentary_election "2024 Icelandic parliamentary election"), the Icelandic government is led by a coalition between the [Social Democratic Alliance](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Alliance "Social Democratic Alliance"), the centrist [Viðreisn](/wiki/Vi%C3%B0reisn "Viðreisn"), and the populist [People's Party](/wiki/People%27s_Party_(Iceland) "People's Party (Iceland)"). The governing coalition's cabinet is led by [Kristrún Frostadóttir](/wiki/Kristr%C3%BAn_Frostad%C3%B3ttir "Kristrún Frostadóttir") of the Social Democrats. Other political parties with seats in the [Althing](/wiki/Althing "Althing") (Parliament) are the center-right [Independence Party](/wiki/Independence_Party_(Iceland) "Independence Party (Iceland)"), the right-wing [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Iceland) "Centre Party (Iceland)"), and the agrarian [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(Iceland) "Progressive Party (Iceland)").

According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Iceland performs in the high range on overall democratic measures, with particular weaknesses in inclusive suffrage.[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114)[[111]](#cite_note-115) In 2024, Iceland was ranked fourth in the strength of its democratic institutions[[112]](#cite_note-116) and 10th in government transparency.[[113]](#cite_note-117) The country has a high level of civic participation, with 81.4% voter turnout during the most recent elections,[[114]](#cite_note-118) compared to an OECD average of 72%. Iceland scored second in Europe for their trust in legal institutions (police, parliament, and judiciary) at a mean of 73% trust as of 2018.[[115]](#cite_note-119) Many political parties remain opposed to EU membership, primarily due to Icelanders' concern about losing control over their natural resources (particularly fisheries).[[116]](#cite_note-120)

### Women's rights

Women in Iceland first gained the right to vote in 1915 (with restrictions) and increased voting rights in 1920.[[117]](#cite_note-121) Iceland was the first country in the world to have a political party formed and led entirely by women.[[118]](#cite_note-122) Known as the [Women's List](/wiki/Women%27s_List "Women's List") (*Kvennalistinn*), it was founded in 1983 to advance the political, economic, and social needs of women. It left a lasting influence on Iceland's politics: every major party has a 40% quota for women. In the 2021 elections, 48% of members of parliament are female compared to the global average of 16% in 2009.[[119]](#cite_note-123)[[120]](#cite_note-124)[[121]](#cite_note-125) [Vigdís Finnbogadóttir](/wiki/Vigd%C3%ADs_Finnbogad%C3%B3ttir "Vigdís Finnbogadóttir") was the world's first democratically elected female head of state. In 2009, [Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir](/wiki/J%C3%B3hanna_Sigur%C3%B0ard%C3%B3ttir "Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir") became the world's [first openly LGBT](/wiki/List_of_the_first_LGBT_holders_of_political_offices "List of the first LGBT holders of political offices") [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[122]](#cite_note-126)

### Government

See also: [Government of Iceland](/wiki/Government_of_Iceland "Government of Iceland")

[![Alþingishúsið](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Al%C3%BEingish%C3%BAsi%C3%B0.jpg/250px-Al%C3%BEingish%C3%BAsi%C3%B0.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al%C3%BEingish%C3%BAsi%C3%B0.jpg)

[Alþingishúsið](/wiki/Al%C3%BEingish%C3%BAsi%C3%B0 "Alþingishúsið") houses the [Althing](/wiki/Althing "Althing") (Parliament of Iceland) in [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík").

[![Stjórnarráðshúsið](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Stj%C3%B3rnarr%C3%A1%C3%B0sh%C3%BAsi%C3%B0_2023.jpg/330px-Stj%C3%B3rnarr%C3%A1%C3%B0sh%C3%BAsi%C3%B0_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stj%C3%B3rnarr%C3%A1%C3%B0sh%C3%BAsi%C3%B0_2023.jpg)

[Stjórnarráðshúsið](/wiki/Stj%C3%B3rnarr%C3%A1%C3%B0sh%C3%BAsi%C3%B0 "Stjórnarráðshúsið") houses the [Cabinet of Iceland](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Iceland "Cabinet of Iceland") and the [Prime Minister's Office](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Iceland "Prime Minister of Iceland") in Reykjavík.

Iceland is a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") and a [parliamentary republic](/wiki/Parliamentary_republic "Parliamentary republic"). The modern parliament, *Alþingi* (English: [Althing](/wiki/Althing "Althing")), was founded in 1845 as an advisory body to the Danish monarch. It was widely seen as a re-establishment of the assembly founded in 930 in the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Icelandic_Commonwealth "Icelandic Commonwealth") period and temporarily suspended from 1799 to 1845. Consequently, "it is arguably the world's oldest [parliamentary democracy](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system")."[[123]](#cite_note-127) It has 63 members, elected for a maximum period of four years.[[124]](#cite_note-128) The head of government is the prime minister, who with the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Iceland "Cabinet of Iceland"), is responsible for [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") government.

The [president of Iceland](/wiki/President_of_Iceland "President of Iceland"), in contrast, is a largely ceremonial head of state and serves as a diplomat, but may veto laws voted by the parliament and put them to a national referendum.[[125]](#cite_note-129)[[126]](#cite_note-130) They are elected by popular vote for a term of four years with no term limit. The current president is [Halla Tómasdóttir](/wiki/Halla_T%C3%B3masd%C3%B3ttir "Halla Tómasdóttir"), having been in office since 1 August 2024. The elections for the president, the Althing, and local municipal councils are all held separately every four years.[[127]](#cite_note-131)

The cabinet in the country's government is typically appointed by the president after a general election to the Althing. However, the appointment is usually negotiated by the leaders of the political parties, who decide amongst themselves which parties can form the cabinet and how to distribute its seats, as long as it has majority support in the Althing. If the party leaders are unable to come to an agreement within a reasonable period of time, the president will personally appoint the cabinet. That has not happened since the republic was founded in 1944, although in 1942 the regent, [Sveinn Björnsson](/wiki/Sveinn_Bj%C3%B6rnsson "Sveinn Björnsson"), appointed a non-parliamentary government. Sveinn held the practical position of president at the time and later became the country's first official president in 1944.

The governments of Iceland have always been coalition governments, with two or more parties involved, as no single political party has ever received a majority of seats in the Althing throughout the republican period. There is no legal consensus on the extent of the political power possessed by the office of the president; several provisions of the constitution appear to give the president some important powers, but other provisions and traditions suggest otherwise.[[128]](#cite_note-132) In 1980, Icelanders elected [Vigdís Finnbogadóttir](/wiki/Vigd%C3%ADs_Finnbogad%C3%B3ttir "Vigdís Finnbogadóttir") as president, the world's first directly elected female head of state. She retired from office in 1996. In 2009, Iceland became the first country with an openly gay head of government when [Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir](/wiki/J%C3%B3hanna_Sigur%C3%B0ard%C3%B3ttir "Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir") became prime minister.[[129]](#cite_note-133)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Iceland](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Iceland "Administrative divisions of Iceland")

Iceland is divided into regions, constituencies, and municipalities. The eight regions are primarily used for statistical purposes. District court jurisdictions also use an older version of this division.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79) Until 2003, the constituencies for the parliamentary elections were the same as the regions, but by an amendment to the constitution, they were changed to the current six constituencies:

* *[Reykjavík North](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Iceland "Constituencies of Iceland")* and *[Reykjavík South](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Iceland "Constituencies of Iceland")* (city regions)
* *[Southwest](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Iceland "Constituencies of Iceland")* (four [non-contiguous](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") suburban areas around Reykjavík)
* *[Northwest](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Iceland "Constituencies of Iceland")* and *[Northeast](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Iceland "Constituencies of Iceland")* (northern half of Iceland, split)
* *[South](/wiki/Constituencies_of_Iceland "Constituencies of Iceland")* (southern half of Iceland, excluding Reykjavík and suburbs)

The redistricting change was made to balance the weight of different districts of the country, since previously a vote cast in the sparsely populated areas around the country would count much more than a vote cast in the Reykjavík city area. The imbalance between districts has been reduced by the new system, but it still exists.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79)

Sixty-two municipalities in Iceland govern local matters like schools, transport, and zoning.[[130]](#cite_note-134) These are the actual [second-level subdivisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") of Iceland, as the constituencies have no relevance except in elections and for statistical purposes. Reykjavík is by far the most populous municipality, about four times more populous than [Kópavogur](/wiki/K%C3%B3pavogur "Kópavogur"), the second largest.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79)
