### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Iceland](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Iceland "Foreign relations of Iceland")

See also: [Accession of Iceland to the European Union](/wiki/Accession_of_Iceland_to_the_European_Union "Accession of Iceland to the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Barack_Obama_and_the_Nordic_leaders_at_White_House_01.jpg/250px-Barack_Obama_and_the_Nordic_leaders_at_White_House_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_and_the_Nordic_leaders_at_White_House_01.jpg)

Nordic prime ministers and the president of Finland visiting the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") in 2016, with Iceland's [Sigurður](/wiki/Sigur%C3%B0ur_Ingi_J%C3%B3hannsson "Sigurður Ingi Jóhannsson") second from the left

Iceland, which is a member of the [UN](/wiki/UN "UN"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), [EFTA](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association"), [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), and [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), maintains diplomatic and commercial relations with practically all nations, but its ties with the Nordic countries, Germany, the United States, Canada, and the other NATO nations are particularly close. Historically, due to cultural, economic, and linguistic similarities, Iceland is a [Nordic country](/wiki/Nordic_country "Nordic country"), and it participates in intergovernmental cooperation through the [Nordic Council](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council").

Iceland is a member of the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") (EEA), which allows the country access to the single market of the European Union (EU). It was not a member of the EU, but in July 2009, the Icelandic parliament, the Althing, voted in favour of the application for EU membership[[131]](#cite_note-135) and officially applied on 17 July 2009.[[132]](#cite_note-136) However, in 2013, opinion polls showed that many Icelanders were now against joining the EU; after the [2013 Icelandic parliamentary election](/wiki/2013_Icelandic_parliamentary_election "2013 Icelandic parliamentary election") the two parties which formed the island's new government—the centrist Progressive Party and the right-wing Independence Party—announced that they would hold a referendum on EU membership.[[133]](#cite_note-137) In 2015, Minister for Foreign Affairs [Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson](/wiki/Gunnar_Bragi_Sveinsson "Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson") informed the EU that Iceland would no longer pursue membership, but the application was not formally withdrawn and there have been subsequent calls for a referendum on the issue.[[134]](#cite_note-138)[[135]](#cite_note-139)

### Military

Main article: [Defence of Iceland](/wiki/Defence_of_Iceland "Defence of Iceland")

Iceland has no [standing army](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") but has the [Icelandic Coast Guard](/wiki/Icelandic_Coast_Guard "Icelandic Coast Guard"), which also maintains the [Iceland Air Defence System](/wiki/Iceland_Air_Defence_System "Iceland Air Defence System") and an [Iceland Crisis Response Unit](/wiki/Iceland_Crisis_Response_Unit "Iceland Crisis Response Unit") to support peacekeeping missions and perform paramilitary functions.

The [Iceland Defense Force](/wiki/Iceland_Defense_Force "Iceland Defense Force") (IDF) was a [military command](/wiki/Command_(military_formation) "Command (military formation)") of the [United States Armed Forces](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") from 1951 to 2006. The IDF, created at the request of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), came into existence when the United States signed an agreement to provide for the defense of Iceland. The IDF also consisted of civilian Icelanders and military members of other NATO nations. The IDF was downsized after the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), and the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") maintained four to six [interceptor aircraft](/wiki/Interceptor_aircraft "Interceptor aircraft") at the [Naval Air Station Keflavik](/wiki/Naval_Air_Station_Keflavik "Naval Air Station Keflavik") until they were withdrawn on 30 September 2006. Since May 2008, NATO nations have periodically deployed fighters to patrol Icelandic airspace under the [Icelandic Air Policing](/wiki/Icelandic_Air_Policing "Icelandic Air Policing") mission.[[136]](#cite_note-French_Air_Force_in_Iceland-140)[[137]](#cite_note-NATO_ACO-141) Iceland supported the [2003 invasion of Iraq](/wiki/2003_invasion_of_Iraq "2003 invasion of Iraq") despite much domestic controversy, deploying a Coast Guard [EOD](/wiki/Explosive_Ordnance_Disposal "Explosive Ordnance Disposal") team to Iraq,[[138]](#cite_note-142) which was replaced later by members of the [Iceland Crisis Response Unit](/wiki/Iceland_Crisis_Response_Unit "Iceland Crisis Response Unit"). Iceland has also participated in the [conflict in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)")[[139]](#cite_note-143) and the [1999 NATO bombing of Yugoslavia](/wiki/1999_NATO_bombing_of_Yugoslavia "1999 NATO bombing of Yugoslavia").[[140]](#cite_note-144) Despite the ongoing financial crisis the first [new patrol ship](/wiki/ICGV_%C3%9E%C3%B3r_(2009) "ICGV Þór (2009)") in decades was launched on 29 April 2009.[[141]](#cite_note-145)

Iceland was the neutral host of the historic 1986 [Reagan–Gorbachev summit](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_Summit "Reykjavík Summit") in Reykjavík, which set the stage for the end of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). Iceland's principal historical international disputes involved disagreements over [exclusive economic zones](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone"). Conflict with the United Kingdom led to a series of so-called [Cod Wars](/wiki/Cod_Wars "Cod Wars"), which included confrontations between the Icelandic Coast Guard and the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") regarding British fishermen: in 1952–1956 due to the extension of Iceland's fishing zone from 3 to 4 [nmi](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") (5.6 to 7.4 km; 3.5 to 4.6 mi), in 1958–1961 following a further extension to 12 nmi (22.2 km; 13.8 mi), in 1972–1973 with another extension to 50 nmi (92.6 km; 57.5 mi), and in 1975–1976 after another extension to 200 nmi (370.4 km; 230.2 mi).[[142]](#cite_note-146)

According to the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index"), Iceland is the most peaceful country in the world, due to its lack of armed forces, low crime rate, and high level of socio-political stability.[[143]](#cite_note-147) Iceland is listed in *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as the "country ranked most at peace"[[144]](#cite_note-148) and the "lowest military spending per capita".[[145]](#cite_note-149)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Iceland](/wiki/Economy_of_Iceland "Economy of Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/%D0%90%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%80%D1%96_%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D1%83%2C_%D0%86%D1%81%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%96%D1%8F.jpg/250px-%D0%90%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%80%D1%96_%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D1%83%2C_%D0%86%D1%81%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%96%D1%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%90%D0%BA%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%80%D1%96_%D0%B2%D0%BB%D1%96%D1%82%D0%BA%D1%83,_%D0%86%D1%81%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%96%D1%8F.jpg)

[Akureyri](/wiki/Akureyri "Akureyri") is the largest town in Iceland outside the [Capital Region](/wiki/Capital_Region_(Iceland) "Capital Region (Iceland)"). Most rural towns are based on the fishing industry, which provides 40% of Iceland's exports.

In 2022, Iceland was the [eighth-most productive country in the world per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita") (US$78,837), and the thirteenth-most productive by [GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") at [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") ($69,833).[[146]](#cite_note-150) About 85 percent of the total [primary energy](/wiki/Primary_energy "Primary energy") supply in Iceland is derived from domestically produced renewable energy sources.[[147]](#cite_note-151) Use of abundant [hydroelectric](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") and [geothermal power](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power") has made Iceland the world's largest electricity producer per capita.[[148]](#cite_note-largest-electricity-152)

Historically, Iceland's economy depended heavily on fishing, which still provides ~20% of export earnings and employs 7% of the workforce.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79)[[149]](#cite_note-:2-153) The economy is now more dependent on tourism, but important sectors continue to be fish and fish products, aluminium, and [ferrosilicon](/wiki/Ferrosilicon "Ferrosilicon"). Iceland's economic dependence on fishing is diminishing, from an export share of 90% in the 1960s to 20% in 2020.[[150]](#cite_note-154)[[149]](#cite_note-:2-153)

Until the 20th century, Iceland was a fairly poor country. [Whaling in Iceland](/wiki/Whaling_in_Iceland "Whaling in Iceland") was historically significant. It is now one of the most developed countries in the world. Strong economic growth led Iceland to be ranked [third](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of countries by Human Development Index") in the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")' [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") report for 2021/2022.[[9]](#cite_note-HDI-11) According to the Economist Intelligence Index of 2011, Iceland had the second-highest quality of life in the world.[[151]](#cite_note-155) Based on the [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient"), Iceland also has one of the lowest rates of income inequality in the world,[[152]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-156) and when [adjusted for inequality](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_inequality-adjusted_HDI "List of countries by inequality-adjusted HDI"), its HDI ranking is sixth.[[153]](#cite_note-157) Iceland's unemployment rate has declined consistently since the crisis, with 4.8% of the labour force being unemployed as of June 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit), compared to 6% in 2011 and 8.1% in 2010.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79)[[154]](#cite_note-158)[[155]](#cite_note-159)

The national currency of Iceland is the [Icelandic króna](/wiki/Icelandic_kr%C3%B3na "Icelandic króna") (ISK). Iceland is the only country in the world to have a population under two million yet still have a floating exchange rate and an independent monetary policy.[[156]](#cite_note-160) A poll released on 5 March 2010 by Capacent [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_(company) "Gallup (company)") showed that 31% of respondents were in favor of adopting the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") and 69% opposed.[[157]](#cite_note-161) Another Capacent Gallup poll conducted in February 2012 discovered that 67.4% of Icelanders would reject EU membership in a referendum.[[158]](#cite_note-162)

Iceland's economy has been diversifying into manufacturing and service industries in the last decade, including software production, [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), and finance; industry accounts for around a quarter of economic activity, while services comprise close to 70%.[[159]](#cite_note-163) The tourism sector is expanding, especially in [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") and whale-watching. On average, Iceland receives around 1.1 million visitors annually, which is more than three times the native population.[[160]](#cite_note-Iceland_–_OECD_Better_Life_Index-164) 1.7 million people visited Iceland in 2016, 3 times more than the number that came in 2010.[[161]](#cite_note-165) Iceland's agriculture industry, accounting for 5.4% of GDP,[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79) consists mainly of [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), green vegetables (in [greenhouses](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse")), [mutton](/wiki/Mutton "Mutton"), and dairy products.[[75]](#cite_note-CIA_Govt-79) The financial center is [Borgartún](/wiki/Borgart%C3%BAn "Borgartún") in Reykjavík, which hosts a large number of companies and three investment banks. Iceland's [stock market](/wiki/Stock_market "Stock market"), the [Iceland Stock Exchange](/wiki/Iceland_Stock_Exchange "Iceland Stock Exchange") (ISE), was established in 1985.[[162]](#cite_note-166) As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit), Iceland ranks 26th in the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum")'s Global Competitive Index, two places lower than in 2018.[[163]](#cite_note-167) According to the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index"), Iceland is the 22nd most innovative country in the world in 2024.[[164]](#cite_note-168)

Despite low tax rates, agricultural assistance is the highest among OECD countries and a potential impediment to structural change. Also, health care and education spending have relatively poor returns by OECD measures, though improvements have been made in both areas. The OECD's *Economic Survey of Iceland 2008* highlighted Iceland's challenges in currency and macroeconomic policy.[[165]](#cite_note-169) There was a [currency crisis](/wiki/Currency_crisis "Currency crisis") that started in the spring of 2008, and on 6 October trading in Iceland's banks was suspended as the government battled to save the economy.[[166]](#cite_note-170) An assessment by the OECD 2011[[167]](#cite_note-oecd2011-171) determined that Iceland has made progress in many areas, particularly in creating a sustainable fiscal policy and restoring the health of the financial sector; however, challenges remain in making the fishing industry more efficient and sustainable, as well as in improving monetary policy to address inflation.[[167]](#cite_note-oecd2011-171) Iceland's public debt has decreased since the economic crisis, and as of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit) is the 31st-highest in the world by proportion of national GDP.[[168]](#cite_note-172)

### Economic contraction

Main article: [2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/W16_Protesters_2659.JPG/250px-W16_Protesters_2659.JPG)](/wiki/File:W16_Protesters_2659.JPG)

[2009 Icelandic financial crisis protests](/wiki/2009_Icelandic_financial_crisis_protests "2009 Icelandic financial crisis protests")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Iceland_bonds.webp/500px-Iceland_bonds.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Iceland_bonds.webp)

Iceland bonds had an [inverted yield curve](/wiki/Inverted_yield_curve "Inverted yield curve") in 2008:

10 year bonds

5 year bonds

2 year bonds

Iceland was hit especially hard by the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") that began in December 2007 because of the failure of its banking system and a subsequent economic crisis. Before the crash of the country's three largest banks, [Glitnir](/wiki/Glitnir_(bank) "Glitnir (bank)"), [Landsbanki](/wiki/Landsbanki "Landsbanki"), and [Kaupthing](/wiki/Kaupthing "Kaupthing"), their combined debt exceeded approximately six times the nation's gross domestic product of €14 billion ($19 billion).[[169]](#cite_note-bbc.1-173)[[170]](#cite_note-174) In October 2008, the Icelandic parliament passed emergency legislation to minimise the impact of the financial crisis. The Financial Supervisory Authority of Iceland used the permission granted by the emergency legislation to take over the domestic operations of the three largest banks.[[171]](#cite_note-175) Icelandic officials, including central bank governor [Davíð Oddsson](/wiki/Dav%C3%AD%C3%B0_Oddsson "Davíð Oddsson"), stated that the state did not intend to take over any of the banks' foreign debts or assets. Instead, new banks were established to take on the domestic operations of the banks, and the old banks were to be run into bankruptcy.

On 28 October 2008, the Icelandic government raised interest rates to 18% (as of August 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit), it was 3.5%), a move forced in part by the terms of acquiring a loan from [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF). After the rate hike, trading on the Icelandic króna finally resumed on the open market, with a valuation at around 250 ISK per euro, less than one-third the value of the 1:70 exchange rate during most of 2008, and a significant drop from the 1:150 exchange ratio of the week before. On 20 November 2008, the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") agreed to lend Iceland $2.5 billion.[[172]](#cite_note-176)

On 26 January 2009, the coalition government collapsed due to public dissent over the handling of the financial crisis. A new left-wing government was formed a week later and immediately set about removing Central Bank governor Davíð Oddsson and his aides from the bank through changes in the law. Davíð was removed on 26 February 2009 in the wake of protests outside the Central Bank.[[173]](#cite_note-177)

Thousands of Icelanders left the country after the collapse, many of them moved to Norway. In 2005, 293 people moved from Iceland to Norway; in 2009, the figure was 1,625.[[174]](#cite_note-178) In April 2010, the Icelandic Parliament's Special Investigation Commission published the findings of its investigation,[[175]](#cite_note-179) revealing the extent of control fraud in this crisis.[[176]](#cite_note-180) By June 2012, Landsbanki managed to repay about half of the [Icesave](/wiki/Icesave_dispute "Icesave dispute") debt.[[177]](#cite_note-181) According to [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P.") in 2014, Iceland was on the trajectory of 2% unemployment as a result of crisis-management decisions made back in 2008, including allowing the banks to fail.[[178]](#cite_note-182)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Iceland](/wiki/Transport_in_Iceland "Transport in Iceland")

#### Road

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Route1%28iceland%29.png/250px-Route1%28iceland%29.png)](/wiki/File:Route1(iceland).png)

[The Ring Road of Iceland](/wiki/Route_1_(Iceland) "Route 1 (Iceland)") and some towns it passes through: 1. [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), 2. [Borgarnes](/wiki/Borgarnes "Borgarnes"), 3. [Blönduós](/wiki/Bl%C3%B6ndu%C3%B3s "Blönduós"), 4. [Akureyri](/wiki/Akureyri "Akureyri"), 5. [Egilsstaðir](/wiki/Egilssta%C3%B0ir "Egilsstaðir"), 6. [Höfn](/wiki/H%C3%B6fn "Höfn"), 7. [Selfoss](/wiki/Selfoss_(town) "Selfoss (town)")

Iceland has a high level of car ownership per capita, with a car for every 1.5 inhabitants; it is the main form of transport.[[179]](#cite_note-183) Iceland has 13,034 km (8,099 mi) of administered roads, of which 4,617 km (2,869 mi) are paved and 8,338 km (5,181 mi) are not.[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] The road speed limits are 30 and 50 km/h (19 and 31 mph) in towns, 80 km/h (50 mph) on gravel country roads and 90 km/h (56 mph) on hard-surfaced roads. A great number of interior roads remain unpaved, mostly little-used rural roads.[[180]](#cite_note-184) Many of Iceland's roads can be challenging to navigate during winter, particularly when covered with snow or ice. Using a 4x4 vehicle provides greater flexibility for travel and improves accessibility to remote areas.[[181]](#cite_note-185)

[Route 1](/wiki/Route_1_(Iceland) "Route 1 (Iceland)"), or the Ring Road ([Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"): *Þjóðvegur 1* or *Hringvegur*), completed in 1974, is the main road that runs around Iceland and connects most inhabited parts of the island. The interior of the island is mostly uninhabitable. The road is paved and is 1,332 km (828 mi) long with one lane in each direction, except between and within larger towns and cities where it has more lanes.[[182]](#cite_note-186) On Route 1, there are about 30 single-lane bridges, particularly prevalent in the southeast.[[183]](#cite_note-187)

#### Public transport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Str%C3%A6t%C3%B3_AI_H01_%2827837800934%29.jpg/250px-Str%C3%A6t%C3%B3_AI_H01_%2827837800934%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Str%C3%A6t%C3%B3_AI_H01_(27837800934).jpg)

Long-distance bus operated by the public bus company [Strætó](/wiki/Str%C3%A6t%C3%B3_bs "Strætó bs")

[City buses](/wiki/Bus "Bus") in [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík") (including the [Capital Region](/wiki/Capital_Region_(Iceland) "Capital Region (Iceland)")) are operated by [Strætó bs](/wiki/Str%C3%A6t%C3%B3_bs "Strætó bs"). Long-distance public bus services throughout the country are also provided by [Strætó bs](/wiki/Str%C3%A6t%C3%B3_bs "Strætó bs"). Smaller towns such as [Akureyri](/wiki/Akureyri "Akureyri"), [Reykjanesbær](/wiki/Reykjanesb%C3%A6r "Reykjanesbær") and [Selfoss](/wiki/Selfoss_(town) "Selfoss (town)") provide local bus services.[[184]](#cite_note-188) Public and private bus services are available to and from Keflavik International Airport.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

No passenger railways have ever operated in Iceland. Previously, temporary freight [railways have operated in Iceland](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Iceland "Rail transport in Iceland").

#### Air travel

[Keflavík International Airport](/wiki/Keflav%C3%ADk_International_Airport "Keflavík International Airport") (KEF)[[186]](#cite_note-190) is the largest airport and the main aviation hub for international passenger transport.[[187]](#cite_note-191) KEF is in the southwest of the country, 49 km (30 mi)[[188]](#cite_note-192) from Reykjavík City Center. [Reykjavík Airport](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_Airport "Reykjavík Airport") (RKV)[[189]](#cite_note-193) is the second-largest airport, located just 1.5 km from the capital centre. Reykjavík Airport serves daily regular domestic flights within Iceland, general aviation, private aviation, and [medivac](/wiki/Medical_evacuation "Medical evacuation") traffic.[[190]](#cite_note-194)

[Akureyri Airport](/wiki/Akureyri_Airport "Akureyri Airport") (AEY)[[191]](#cite_note-195) and [Egilsstaðir Airport](/wiki/Egilssta%C3%B0ir_Airport "Egilsstaðir Airport") (EGS)[[192]](#cite_note-196) are two other airports with domestic service and limited international service. Akureyri Airport opened an expanded international terminal in 2024.[[193]](#cite_note-197) There are a total of 103 registered airports and airfields in Iceland; most of them are unpaved and located in rural areas.

#### Sea

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Norr%C3%B6na_in_Seydisfj%C3%B6rdur-pjt.jpg/250px-Norr%C3%B6na_in_Seydisfj%C3%B6rdur-pjt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norr%C3%B6na_in_Seydisfj%C3%B6rdur-pjt.jpg)

Norröna ferry in Seyðisfjorður, bound to the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands") and Denmark

Several ferry services provide regular access to various island communities and shorten travel distances. The [Smyril Line](/wiki/Smyril_Line "Smyril Line") operates the ship [Norröna](/wiki/MS_Norr%C3%B6na "MS Norröna") providing an international ferry service from [Seyðisfjörður](/wiki/Sey%C3%B0isfj%C3%B6r%C3%B0ur "Seyðisfjörður") to the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands") and [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark").[[194]](#cite_note-198) Several companies including [Eimskip](/wiki/Eimskip "Eimskip") and Samskip provide maritime transport services to Iceland and Iceland's largest ports are managed by Faxaflóahafnir.[[195]](#cite_note-199)

### Energy

See also: [Energy in Iceland](/wiki/Energy_in_Iceland "Energy in Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg/250px-NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg)](/wiki/File:NesjavellirPowerPlant_edit2.jpg)

The [Nesjavellir Geothermal Power Station](/wiki/Nesjavellir_Geothermal_Power_Station "Nesjavellir Geothermal Power Station") services the [Capital Region](/wiki/Capital_Region_(Iceland) "Capital Region (Iceland)")'s hot water and electricity needs. Virtually all of Iceland's electricity comes from renewable resources.[[196]](#cite_note-energy-200)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Iceland_electricity_production.svg/250px-Iceland_electricity_production.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Iceland_electricity_production.svg)

Iceland electricity production by source

[Renewable sources](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy")—[geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power") and [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower")—provide effectively all of Iceland's electricity[[196]](#cite_note-energy-200) and around 85% of the nation's total primary energy consumption,[[197]](#cite_note-201) with most of the remainder consisting of imported oil products used in transportation and in the fishing fleet.[[198]](#cite_note-statice1-202)[[199]](#cite_note-203) Iceland's largest geothermal power plants are [Hellisheiði](/wiki/Hellishei%C3%B0i_Power_Station "Hellisheiði Power Station") and [Nesjavellir](/wiki/Nesjavellir_Geothermal_Power_Station "Nesjavellir Geothermal Power Station"),[[200]](#cite_note-204)[[201]](#cite_note-205) while [Kárahnjúkar Hydropower Plant](/wiki/K%C3%A1rahnj%C3%BAkar_Hydropower_Plant "Kárahnjúkar Hydropower Plant") is the country's largest hydroelectric power station.[[202]](#cite_note-206) When the Kárahnjúkavirkjun began operating, Iceland became the world's largest electricity producer per capita.[[148]](#cite_note-largest-electricity-152) In 2023, [battery electric vehicles](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle") constituted 50.1% of new registrations, and around 18% of the country's vehicle fleet was electrified in 2024.[[203]](#cite_note-207)[[204]](#cite_note-208) Iceland is one of the few countries that have filling stations dispensing hydrogen fuel for cars powered by [fuel cells](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell").[[205]](#cite_note-209)

Despite that, Icelanders emitted 16.9 tonnes of CO2 per capita in 2016, the highest among EFTA and EU members, mainly resulting from transport and [aluminium smelting](/wiki/Aluminium_smelting "Aluminium smelting").[[206]](#cite_note-210) Nevertheless, in 2010, Iceland was reported by *Guinness World Records* as "the Greenest Country", reaching the highest score by the [Environmental Sustainability Index](/wiki/Environmental_Sustainability_Index "Environmental Sustainability Index"), which measures a country's water use, biodiversity and adoption of clean energies, with a score of 93.5/100.[[207]](#cite_note-211)

On 22 January 2009, Iceland announced its first round of [offshore](/wiki/Oil_platform "Oil platform") licences for companies wanting to conduct [hydrocarbon exploration](/wiki/Hydrocarbon_exploration "Hydrocarbon exploration") and production in a region northeast of Iceland, known as the [Dreki area](/wiki/Jan_Mayen_Microcontinent#Economic_resources "Jan Mayen Microcontinent").[[208]](#cite_note-212) Three exploration licenses were awarded but all were later relinquished.[[209]](#cite_note-213)

Iceland's official governmental goal is to cut [greenhouse gas](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") emissions by 40% by the year 2030 and reach [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") by the year 2040.[[210]](#cite_note-214) As a result of its commitment to renewable energy, the 2016 Global Green Economy Index ranked Iceland among the top 10 greenest economies in the world.[[211]](#cite_note-ggei-215)

### Education and science

See also: [Education in Iceland](/wiki/Education_in_Iceland "Education in Iceland") and [Science and technology in Iceland](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Iceland "Science and technology in Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Menntask%C3%B3linn_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk_%28main_building%2C_2004%29.jpg/250px-Menntask%C3%B3linn_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk_%28main_building%2C_2004%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Menntask%C3%B3linn_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk_(main_building,_2004).jpg)

Reykjavík Junior College (*[Menntaskólinn í Reykjavík](/wiki/Menntask%C3%B3linn_%C3%AD_Reykjav%C3%ADk "Menntaskólinn í Reykjavík")*), in downtown Reykjavík, is the oldest [gymnasium](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") in Iceland.

The [Ministry of Education, Science and Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education,_Science_and_Culture_(Iceland) "Ministry of Education, Science and Culture (Iceland)") is responsible for the policies and methods that schools must use, and they issue the National Curriculum Guidelines. However, playschools, primary schools, and lower secondary schools are funded and administered by the municipalities. The government allows citizens to [home educate](/wiki/Homeschooling "Homeschooling") their children, however, under a very strict set of demands.[[212]](#cite_note-216) Students must adhere closely to the government-mandated curriculum, and the parents teaching must acquire a government-approved teaching certificate.

Nursery school, or *leikskóli*, is non-compulsory education for children younger than six years and is the first step in the education system. The current legislation concerning playschools was passed in 1994. They are also responsible for ensuring that the curriculum is suitable to make the transition into compulsory education as easy as possible.[[213]](#cite_note-217)[[214]](#cite_note-218)[[215]](#cite_note-219)

Compulsory education, or *grunnskóli*, comprises primary and lower secondary education, which is often conducted at the same institution. Education is mandatory by law for children aged from 6 to 16 years. The school year lasts nine months, beginning between 21 August and 1 September, and ending between 31 May and 10 June. The minimum number of school days was once 170, but after a new teachers' wage contract, it increased to 180. Lessons take place five days a week. All public schools have mandatory education in Christianity, although an exemption may be considered by the [Minister of Education](/wiki/Minister_of_Education,_Science_and_Culture_(Iceland) "Minister of Education, Science and Culture (Iceland)").[[216]](#cite_note-Jonathan_Fox-220)

Upper secondary education, or *framhaldsskóli*, follows lower secondary education. These schools are also known as [gymnasia](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") in English. Though not compulsory, everyone who has had a compulsory education has the right to upper secondary education. This stage of education is governed by the Upper Secondary School Act of 1996. All schools in Iceland are mixed-sex schools. The largest seat of higher education is the [University of Iceland](/wiki/University_of_Iceland "University of Iceland"), which has its main campus in central Reykjavík. Other schools offering university-level instruction include [Reykjavík University](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_University "Reykjavík University"), [University of Akureyri](/wiki/University_of_Akureyri "University of Akureyri"), [Agricultural University of Iceland](/wiki/Agricultural_University_of_Iceland "Agricultural University of Iceland"), and [Bifröst University](/wiki/Bifr%C3%B6st_University "Bifröst University"). An OECD assessment found that 64% of Icelanders aged 25–64 have earned the equivalent of a high-school degree, which is lower than the OECD average of 73%. Among 25- to 34-year-olds, only 69% have earned the equivalent of a high-school degree, significantly lower than the OECD average of 80%.[[160]](#cite_note-Iceland_–_OECD_Better_Life_Index-164) Nevertheless, Iceland's education system is considered excellent: the [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") ranks it as the 16th best performing, above the OECD average.[[217]](#cite_note-221) Students were particularly proficient in reading and mathematics.

According to a 2013 [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") report by the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), Iceland spends around 3.11% of its GDP on scientific research and development (R&D), over 1 percentage point higher than the EU average of 2.03%, and has set a target of 4% to reach by 2020.[[218]](#cite_note-222) Iceland was ranked 24th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025[[219]](#cite_note-223)[[220]](#cite_note-224) A 2010 [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") report found that out of 72 countries that spend the most on R&D (US$100 million or more), Iceland ranked ninth by proportion of GDP, tied with Taiwan, Switzerland, and Germany and ahead of France, the United Kingdom and Canada.[[221]](#cite_note-225)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Iceland](/wiki/Demographics_of_Iceland "Demographics of Iceland")

See also: [Icelanders](/wiki/Icelanders "Icelanders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Reykjavik_City_Iceland_Downtown_Cityscape.jpg/250px-Reykjavik_City_Iceland_Downtown_Cityscape.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reykjavik_City_Iceland_Downtown_Cityscape.jpg)

[Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), Iceland's largest metropolitan area and the centre of the [Capital Region](/wiki/Capital_Region_(Iceland) "Capital Region (Iceland)") which, with a population of 233,034, makes up 64% of Iceland's population (numbers from 2020)

The original population of Iceland was of [Norse](/wiki/Northmen "Northmen") and [Gaelic](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels") origin which is evident from literary evidence dating from the settlement period as well as from later scientific studies such as [blood type](/wiki/Blood_type "Blood type") and genetic analyses. One such genetic study indicated that the majority of the male settlers were of Nordic origin, while the majority of the women were of Gaelic origin, meaning many settlers of Iceland were Norsemen who brought Gaelic slaves with them.[[222]](#cite_note-226)

Iceland has extensive genealogical records dating back to the late 17th century and fragmentary records extending back to the [Age of Settlement](/wiki/Settlement_of_Iceland "Settlement of Iceland"). The biopharmaceutical company [deCODE genetics](/wiki/DeCODE_genetics "DeCODE genetics") has funded the creation of a [genealogy](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy") database that is intended to cover all of Iceland's known inhabitants. It views the database, called *[Íslendingabók](/wiki/%C3%8Dslendingab%C3%B3k_(genealogical_database) "Íslendingabók (genealogical database)")*, as a valuable tool for conducting research on genetic diseases, given the relative isolation of Iceland's population.

The population of the island is believed to have varied from 40,000 to 60,000 in the period ranging from initial settlement until the mid-19th century. During that time, cold winters, ash fall from volcanic eruptions, and [bubonic plagues](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") adversely affected the population several times.[[13]](#cite_note-tomasson-17) There were 37 years of [famine](/wiki/Famine "Famine") in Iceland between 1500 and 1804.[[223]](#cite_note-227) The first census was carried out in 1703 and revealed that the population was then 50,358. After the destructive volcanic eruptions of the [Laki volcano](/wiki/Laki "Laki") during 1783–1784, the population reached a low of about 40,000.[[224]](#cite_note-228) Improving living conditions have triggered a rapid increase in population since the mid-19th century, from about 60,000 in 1850 to 320,000 in 2008. Iceland has a relatively young population for a developed country, with one out of five people being 14 years old or younger. With a fertility rate of 2.1, Iceland is one of only a few European countries with a birth rate [sufficient for long-term population growth](/wiki/Replacement_fertility_rate "Replacement fertility rate") (see table below).[[225]](#cite_note-229)[[226]](#cite_note-230)

In December 2007, 33,678 people (13.5% of the total population) living in Iceland had been born abroad, including children of Icelandic parents living abroad. Around 19,000 people (6% of the population) held foreign citizenship. [Polish](/wiki/Polish_diaspora "Polish diaspora") people make up the largest minority group by a considerable margin and still form the bulk of the foreign workforce.[[1]](#cite_note-citizenship-2) About 8,000 Poles now live in Iceland, 1,500 of them in [Fjarðabyggð](/wiki/Fjar%C3%B0abygg%C3%B0 "Fjarðabyggð"), where they make up 75% of the workforce who are constructing the Fjarðarál aluminium plant.[[227]](#cite_note-231) Large-scale construction projects in the east of Iceland (see [Kárahnjúkar Hydropower Plant](/wiki/K%C3%A1rahnj%C3%BAkar_Hydropower_Plant "Kárahnjúkar Hydropower Plant")) have also brought in many people whose stay is expected to be temporary. Many Polish immigrants were also considering leaving in 2008 as a result of the [Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis").[[228]](#cite_note-232)

The southwest corner of Iceland is by far the most densely populated region. It is also the location of the capital Reykjavík, the northernmost national capital in the world. More than 70 percent of Iceland's population lives in the southwest corner ([Greater Reykjavík](/wiki/Capital_Region_(Iceland) "Capital Region (Iceland)") and the nearby [Southern Peninsula](/wiki/Southern_Peninsula_(Iceland) "Southern Peninsula (Iceland)")), which covers less than two percent of Iceland's land area. The largest town outside Greater Reykjavík is [Reykjanesbær](/wiki/Reykjanesb%C3%A6r "Reykjanesbær"), which is located on the Southern Peninsula, less than 50 km (31 mi) from the capital. The largest town outside the southwest corner is [Akureyri](/wiki/Akureyri "Akureyri") in northern Iceland. About 500 Icelanders under the leadership of [Erik the Red](/wiki/Erik_the_Red "Erik the Red") settled [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland") in the late tenth century.[[229]](#cite_note-233) The total population reached a high point of perhaps 5,000, and developed independent institutions before disappearing by 1500.[[230]](#cite_note-234) People from Greenland attempted to set up a settlement at [Vinland](/wiki/Vinland "Vinland") in North America, but abandoned it in the face of hostility from the [indigenous population](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada").[[231]](#cite_note-235)

Emigration of Icelanders to the United States and Canada began in the 1870s. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit), Canada had over 101,000 people of [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_Canadian "Icelandic Canadian") descent,[[232]](#cite_note-236) while there are more than 40,000 Americans of [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_American "Icelandic American") descent, according to the 2000 US census.[[233]](#cite_note-237)

### Language

Main articles: [Languages of Iceland](/wiki/Languages_of_Iceland "Languages of Iceland") and [Icelandic language](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language")

See also: [Icelandic name](/wiki/Icelandic_name "Icelandic name")

Iceland's official written and spoken language is [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"), a [North Germanic language](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages") descended from [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"). In grammar and vocabulary, it has changed less from Old Norse than the other Nordic languages; Icelandic has preserved more verb and noun [inflection](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection"), and has to a considerable extent developed new vocabulary based on native roots rather than borrowings from other languages. The puristic tendency in the development of Icelandic vocabulary is, to a large degree, a result of conscious language planning, in addition to centuries of isolation. Icelandic is the only living language to retain the use of the [runic](/wiki/Runic "Runic") letter [Þ](/wiki/%C3%9E "Þ") in [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"). The closest living relative of the Icelandic language is [Faroese](/wiki/Faroese_language "Faroese language").

[Icelandic Sign Language](/wiki/Icelandic_Sign_Language "Icelandic Sign Language") was officially recognized as a minority language in 2011. In education, its use for Iceland's deaf community is [regulated](/wiki/Legal_recognition_of_sign_languages "Legal recognition of sign languages") by the *National Curriculum Guide*.

English and Danish are compulsory subjects in the school curriculum. English is widely understood and spoken, while basic to moderate knowledge of Danish is common mainly among the older generations.[[234]](#cite_note-238) [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") is mostly spoken by the local Polish community (the largest minority of Iceland), and Danish is mostly spoken in a way largely comprehensible to Swedes and Norwegians—it is often referred to as *skandinavíska* (i.e. *Scandinavian*) in Iceland.[[235]](#cite_note-239) Iceland was a territory ruled by [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway"), one of whose official languages was Danish; its official status was terminated in 1944.

Rather than using [family names](/wiki/Family_name "Family name"), as is the custom in most Western nations, most Icelanders carry [patronymic](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic") or [matronymic](/wiki/Matronymic "Matronymic") surnames, the usage of patronyms being far more practiced. Patronymic last names are based on the first name of the father, while matronymic names are based on the first name of the mother. They follow the person's given name, e.g. *Elísabet Jónsdóttir* ("Elísabet, Jón's daughter" (Jón being the father)) or *Ólafur Katrínarson* ("Ólafur, Katrín's son" (Katrín being the mother)).[[236]](#cite_note-240) Consequently, Icelanders refer to one another by their given name, and the Icelandic telephone directory lists people alphabetically by the first name rather than by surname.[[237]](#cite_note-241) All new names must be approved by the [Icelandic Naming Committee](/wiki/Icelandic_Naming_Committee "Icelandic Naming Committee").

### Health

Main article: [Healthcare in Iceland](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Iceland "Healthcare in Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Life_expectancy_in_Iceland.svg/250px-Life_expectancy_in_Iceland.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_in_Iceland.svg)

Life expectancy in Iceland, 1838 to 2021

Iceland has a [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") system that is administered by its Ministry of Welfare ([Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"): *Velferðarráðuneytið*)[[238]](#cite_note-242) and paid for mostly by taxes (85%) and to a lesser extent by service fees (15%). Unlike most countries, there are no private hospitals, and private insurance is practically nonexistent.[[239]](#cite_note-j1-243)

A considerable portion of the government budget is assigned to health care,[[239]](#cite_note-j1-243) and Iceland ranks 11th in health care expenditures as a percentage of GDP[[240]](#cite_note-244) and 14th in spending per capita.[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-245) Overall, the country's health care system is one of the best-performing in the world, ranked 15th by the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization").[[242]](#cite_note-246) According to an OECD report, Iceland devotes far more resources to healthcare than most industrialized nations. As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit), Iceland had 3.7 doctors per 1,000 people (compared with an average of 3.1 in OECD countries) and 15.3 nurses per 1,000 people (compared with an OECD average of 8.4).[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-245)

Icelanders are among the world's healthiest people, with 77% reporting they are in good health, more than the OECD average of 68%.[[160]](#cite_note-Iceland_–_OECD_Better_Life_Index-164) Although it is a growing problem, [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity") is not as prevalent as in other [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries").[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-245) Iceland has many campaigns for health and wellbeing, including the famous television show *[LazyTown](/wiki/LazyTown "LazyTown")*, starring and created by former gymnastics champion [Magnus Scheving](/wiki/Magn%C3%BAs_Scheving "Magnús Scheving"). [Infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") is one of the lowest in the world,[[243]](#cite_note-247) and the proportion of the population that smokes is lower than the OECD average.[[241]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-245) Almost all women choose to terminate pregnancies of children with [Down syndrome](/wiki/Down_syndrome "Down syndrome") in Iceland.[[244]](#cite_note-248) The average [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") is 81.8 (compared to an OECD average of 79.5), the fourth-highest in the world.[[245]](#cite_note-249)

Iceland has a very low level of pollution, thanks to an overwhelming reliance on cleaner geothermal energy, a low population density, and a high level of environmental consciousness among [citizens](/wiki/Citizens "Citizens").[[246]](#cite_note-250) According to an OECD assessment, the amount of toxic materials in the atmosphere is far lower than in any other industrialised country measured.[[247]](#cite_note-251) In 2023, the [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide") rate in Iceland was 12.4 cases per 100,000. Over the last five years, from 2019 to 2023, there were an average of 41 suicides per year or 11.3 per 100,000 inhabitants.[[248]](#cite_note-252) In 2022, the country's consumption of antidepressants was the highest in Europe.[[249]](#cite_note-:3-253)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Iceland](/wiki/Religion_in_Iceland "Religion in Iceland")
