[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Sta%C3%B0arbakkakirkja.jpg/250px-Sta%C3%B0arbakkakirkja.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sta%C3%B0arbakkakirkja.jpg)

A church in the northwest of Iceland

Icelanders have freedom of religion guaranteed under the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Iceland "Constitution of Iceland"), although the [Church of Iceland](/wiki/Church_of_Iceland "Church of Iceland"), a [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") body, is the [state church](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"):

> The Evangelical Lutheran Church shall be the State Church in Iceland and, as such, it shall be supported and protected by the State.

— Article 62, Section IV of [Constitution of Iceland](/wiki/Constitution_of_Iceland "Constitution of Iceland")[[251]](#cite_note-255)

Approximately 80 percent of Icelanders legally affiliate with a religious denomination, a process that happens automatically at birth and from which they can choose to opt out. They also pay a church tax (sóknargjald), which the government directs to help support their registered religion, or, in the case of no religion, the University of Iceland.[[252]](#cite_note-:1-256)

The Registers Iceland keeps account of the religious affiliation of every Icelandic citizen. In 2017, Icelanders were divided into religious groups as follows:

* 67.22% members of the [Church of Iceland](/wiki/Church_of_Iceland "Church of Iceland")
* 11.56% members of other [Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Iceland "Christianity in Iceland") denomination
* 11.29% other religions and not specified
* 6.69% unaffiliated
* 1.19% members of [Germanic Heathen](/wiki/Heathenism_(new_religious_movement) "Heathenism (new religious movement)") groups (99% of them belonging to [Ásatrúarfélagið](/wiki/%C3%81satr%C3%BAarf%C3%A9lagi%C3%B0 "Ásatrúarfélagið"))
* 0.67% members of the [Icelandic Ethical Humanist Association](/wiki/Icelandic_Ethical_Humanist_Association "Icelandic Ethical Humanist Association")
* 0.55% members of [Zuist](/wiki/Zuism "Zuism") groups

On 8 March 2021, Iceland formally recognized Judaism as a religion for the first time. Iceland's Jews will have the choice to register as such and direct their taxes to their own religion. Among other benefits, the recognition will also allow Jewish marriage, baby-naming, and funeral ceremonies to be civilly recognized.[[252]](#cite_note-:1-256)

Iceland is a very [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") country; as with other Nordic nations, [church attendance](/wiki/Church_attendance "Church attendance") is relatively low.[[253]](#cite_note-257)[[254]](#cite_note-258) The above statistics represent administrative membership of religious organisations, which does not necessarily reflect the belief demographics of the population. According to a study published in 2001, 23% of the inhabitants were either [atheist](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist") or [agnostic](/wiki/Agnostic "Agnostic").[[255]](#cite_note-259) A Gallup poll conducted in 2012 found that 57% of Icelanders considered themselves "religious", 31% considered themselves "non-religious", while 10% defined themselves as "convinced atheists", placing Iceland among the ten countries with the highest proportion of atheists in the world.[[256]](#cite_note-Religion-and-Atheism-260)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Iceland](/wiki/Culture_of_Iceland "Culture of Iceland")

Icelandic culture has its roots in [North Germanic](/wiki/North_Germanic_peoples "North Germanic peoples") traditions. [Icelandic literature](/wiki/Icelandic_literature "Icelandic literature") is popular, in particular the [sagas](/wiki/Sagas_of_Icelanders "Sagas of Icelanders") and [eddas](/wiki/Edda "Edda") that were written during the [High](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") and [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"). Centuries of isolation have helped to insulate the country's Nordic culture from external influence; a prominent example is the preservation of the [Icelandic language](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"), which remains the closest to Old Norse of all modern Nordic languages.[[257]](#cite_note-261) The country has a strong tradition of upholding [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Iceland "Human rights in Iceland") such as freedom of expression, [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Iceland "Freedom of religion in Iceland") and freedom of assembly with legislation for marginalized groups, such as [women](/wiki/Women%27s_rights_in_Iceland "Women's rights in Iceland"), [immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Iceland "Immigration to Iceland"), and the [LGBTQ+ community](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_Iceland "LGBTQ rights in Iceland").[[258]](#cite_note-c356-262)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Islaendische_Trachten_-_Bilderbuch_zum_Nutzen_und_Vergn%C3%BCgen_der_Jugend.jpg/250px-Islaendische_Trachten_-_Bilderbuch_zum_Nutzen_und_Vergn%C3%BCgen_der_Jugend.jpg)](/wiki/File:Islaendische_Trachten_-_Bilderbuch_zum_Nutzen_und_Vergn%C3%BCgen_der_Jugend.jpg)

[Traditional clothing](/wiki/Icelandic_national_costume "Icelandic national costume") of Icelanders in the early 19th century

In contrast to other Nordic countries, Icelanders place relatively great importance on independence and self-sufficiency; in a public opinion analysis conducted by the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), over 85% of Icelanders believe independence is "very important", compared to 47% of Norwegians, 49% of Danes, and an average of 53% for the EU25.[[259]](#cite_note-europeancommission-263) Icelanders also have a very strong work ethic, working some of the longest hours of any industrialised nation.[[260]](#cite_note-264)

According to a poll conducted by the OECD, 66% of Icelanders were satisfied with their lives, while 70% believed that their lives would be satisfying in the future. Similarly, 83% reported having more positive experiences in an average day than negative ones, compared to an OECD average of 72%, which makes Iceland one of the [happiest countries](/wiki/Happiest_country "Happiest country") in the OECD.[[160]](#cite_note-Iceland_–_OECD_Better_Life_Index-164) A more recent 2012 survey found that around three-quarters of respondents stated they were satisfied with their lives, compared to a global average of about 53%.[[261]](#cite_note-265) In 2022, 2023, and 2024 consecutively, Iceland ranked third in the [World Happiness Report](/wiki/World_Happiness_Report "World Happiness Report").[[249]](#cite_note-:3-253)

Icelanders are known for their strong sense of community and lack of [social isolation](/wiki/Social_isolation "Social isolation"): An OECD survey found that 98% believe they know someone they could rely on in a time of need, higher than in any other industrialized country. Similarly, only 6% reported "rarely" or "never" socialising with others.[[160]](#cite_note-Iceland_–_OECD_Better_Life_Index-164) This high level of social cohesion is attributed to the small size and homogeneity of the population, as well as to a long history of harsh survival in an isolated environment, which reinforced the importance of unity and cooperation.[[262]](#cite_note-266) [Egalitarianism](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") is highly valued among the people of Iceland, with income inequality being among the lowest in the world.[[152]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-156) The constitution explicitly prohibits the enactment of noble privileges, titles, and ranks.[[263]](#cite_note-autogenerated60-267) Everyone is addressed by their first name. As in other Nordic countries, equality between the sexes is very high; Iceland is consistently ranked among the top three countries in the world for women to live in.[[264]](#cite_note-268)[[265]](#cite_note-269)[[266]](#cite_note-270)

### Literature

Main article: [Icelandic literature](/wiki/Icelandic_literature "Icelandic literature")

In 2011, Reykjavík was designated a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") City of Literature.[[267]](#cite_note-auto-271)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/M%C3%B6%C3%B0ruvallab%C3%B3k_f13r.jpg/250px-M%C3%B6%C3%B0ruvallab%C3%B3k_f13r.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%B6%C3%B0ruvallab%C3%B3k_f13r.jpg)

A page of [Njáls saga](/wiki/Nj%C3%A1ls_saga "Njáls saga") from [Möðruvallabók](/wiki/M%C3%B6%C3%B0ruvallab%C3%B3k "Möðruvallabók") is shown. The sagas are a significant part of the Icelandic heritage.

Iceland's best-known classical works of literature are the [Icelanders' sagas](/wiki/Icelanders%27_sagas "Icelanders' sagas"), prose epics set in Iceland's age of settlement. The most famous include *[Njáls saga](/wiki/Nj%C3%A1ls_saga "Njáls saga")*, about an epic blood feud, and *[Grænlendinga saga](/wiki/Gr%C3%A6nlendinga_saga "Grænlendinga saga")* and *[Eiríks saga](/wiki/Saga_of_Erik_the_Red "Saga of Erik the Red")*, describing the discovery and settlement of Greenland and Vinland (modern [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador")). *[Egils saga](/wiki/Egils_saga "Egils saga")*, *[Laxdæla saga](/wiki/Laxd%C3%A6la_saga "Laxdæla saga")*, *[Grettis saga](/wiki/Grettis_saga "Grettis saga")*, *[Gísla saga](/wiki/G%C3%ADsla_saga "Gísla saga")* and *[Gunnlaugs saga ormstungu](/wiki/Gunnlaugs_saga_ormstungu "Gunnlaugs saga ormstungu")* are notable and popular Icelanders' sagas.

A translation of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") was published in the 16th century. Important compositions from the 15th to the 19th century include sacred verse, most famously the [Passion Hymns](/wiki/Passion_Hymns "Passion Hymns") of [Hallgrímur Pétursson](/wiki/Hallgr%C3%ADmur_P%C3%A9tursson "Hallgrímur Pétursson"), and *[rímur](/wiki/R%C3%ADmur "Rímur")*, rhyming epic poems. Originating in the 14th century, *rímur* were popular into the 19th century, when the development of new literary forms was provoked by the influential [National-Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") writer [Jónas Hallgrímsson](/wiki/J%C3%B3nas_Hallgr%C3%ADmsson "Jónas Hallgrímsson"). Since then, Iceland has produced many great writers, the best-known of whom is arguably [Halldór Laxness](/wiki/Halld%C3%B3r_Laxness "Halldór Laxness"), who received the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") in 1955 (the only Icelander to win a Nobel Prize thus far). [Steinn Steinarr](/wiki/Steinn_Steinarr "Steinn Steinarr") was an influential modernist poet during the early 20th century who remains popular.

Icelanders are avid consumers of literature, with the highest number of bookstores per capita in the world. For its size, Iceland imports and translates more international literature than any other nation.[[263]](#cite_note-autogenerated60-267) Iceland also has the highest per capita publication of books and magazines,[[268]](#cite_note-272) and around 10% of the population will publish a book in their lifetimes.[[269]](#cite_note-273)[[270]](#cite_note-274)

Most books in Iceland are sold between late September to early November, a period known as the [Christmas Book Flood](/wiki/Icelandic_Christmas_book_flood "Icelandic Christmas book flood") (*Jólabókaflóð**)*.[[267]](#cite_note-auto-271) The Flood begins with the Iceland Publisher's Association distributing *Bókatíðindi*, a catalogue of all new publications, free to each Icelandic home.[[267]](#cite_note-auto-271)

### Art

Main article: [Icelandic art](/wiki/Icelandic_art "Icelandic art")

The distinctive rendition of the Icelandic landscape by its painters can be linked to nationalism and the movement for [home rule and independence](/wiki/History_of_Iceland#Home_rule_and_sovereignty "History of Iceland"), which was very active in the mid-19th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Thorarinn_thingvellir.jpg/250px-Thorarinn_thingvellir.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thorarinn_thingvellir.jpg)

*[Þingvellir](/wiki/%C3%9Eingvellir "Þingvellir")* by [Þórarinn B. Þorláksson](/wiki/%C3%9E%C3%B3rarinn_B._%C3%9Eorl%C3%A1ksson "Þórarinn B. Þorláksson")

Contemporary Icelandic painting is typically traced to the work of [Þórarinn Þorláksson](/wiki/%C3%9E%C3%B3rarinn_%C3%9Eorl%C3%A1ksson "Þórarinn Þorláksson"), who, following formal training in art in the 1890s in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), returned to Iceland to paint and exhibit works from 1900 to his death in 1924, almost exclusively portraying the Icelandic landscape. Several other Icelandic men and women artists studied at [Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Royal_Danish_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts") at that time, including [Ásgrímur Jónsson](/wiki/%C3%81sgr%C3%ADmur_J%C3%B3nsson "Ásgrímur Jónsson"), who with Þórarinn created a distinctive portrayal of Iceland's landscape in a romantic naturalistic style. Other landscape artists quickly followed in the footsteps of Þórarinn and Ásgrímur. These included [Jóhannes Kjarval](/wiki/J%C3%B3hannes_Sveinsson_Kjarval "Jóhannes Sveinsson Kjarval") and [Júlíana Sveinsdóttir](/wiki/J%C3%BAl%C3%ADana_Sveinsd%C3%B3ttir "Júlíana Sveinsdóttir"). Kjarval, in particular, is noted for the distinct techniques in the application of paint that he developed in a concerted effort to render the characteristic [volcanic rock](/wiki/Volcanic_rock "Volcanic rock") that dominates the Icelandic environment. [Einar Hákonarson](/wiki/Einar_H%C3%A1konarson "Einar Hákonarson") is an expressionistic and figurative painter who, by some, is considered to have brought the figure back into Icelandic painting. In the 1980s, many Icelandic artists worked with the subject of the new painting in their work.

In recent years, the artistic practice has multiplied, and the Icelandic art scene has become a setting for many large-scale projects and exhibitions. The artist-run gallery space Kling og Bang, members of which later ran the studio complex and exhibition venue Klink og Bank, has been a significant part of the trend of self-organized spaces, exhibitions, and projects.[[271]](#cite_note-275) [The Living Art Museum](/wiki/The_Living_Art_Museum "The Living Art Museum"), Reykjavík Municipal Art Museum, [Reykjavík Art Museum](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk_Art_Museum "Reykjavík Art Museum"), and the [National Gallery of Iceland](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Iceland "National Gallery of Iceland") are the larger, more established institutions, curating shows and festivals.

### Music

Main article: [Music of Iceland](/wiki/Music_of_Iceland "Music of Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Bj%C3%B6rk_-_Hurricane_Festival.jpg/250px-Bj%C3%B6rk_-_Hurricane_Festival.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bj%C3%B6rk_-_Hurricane_Festival.jpg)

[Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk") is the best-known Icelandic musician.

Much Icelandic music is related to [Nordic music](/wiki/Nordic_music "Nordic music"), and includes [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [pop](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") traditions. Notable Icelandic music acts include medieval music group [Voces Thules](/wiki/Voces_Thules "Voces Thules"), alternative and indie rock acts such as [The Sugarcubes](/wiki/The_Sugarcubes "The Sugarcubes"), [Sóley](/wiki/S%C3%B3ley "Sóley") and [Of Monsters and Men](/wiki/Of_Monsters_and_Men "Of Monsters and Men"), jazz fusion band [Mezzoforte](/wiki/Mezzoforte_(band) "Mezzoforte (band)"), pop singers such as [Hafdís Huld](/wiki/Hafd%C3%ADs_Huld "Hafdís Huld"), [Emilíana Torrini](/wiki/Emil%C3%ADana_Torrini "Emilíana Torrini"), [Laufey](/wiki/Laufey_(singer) "Laufey (singer)") and [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk"), solo ballad singers like [Bubbi Morthens](/wiki/Bubbi_Morthens "Bubbi Morthens"), and [post-rock](/wiki/Post-rock "Post-rock") bands such as [Amiina](/wiki/Amiina "Amiina") and [Sigur Rós](/wiki/Sigur_R%C3%B3s "Sigur Rós"). [Independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") is strong in Iceland, with bands like [múm](/wiki/M%C3%BAm "Múm") and solo artists such as [Daði Freyr](/wiki/Da%C3%B0i_Freyr "Daði Freyr").

Traditional Icelandic music is strongly religious. Hymns, both religious and secular, are a particularly well-developed form of music, due to the scarcity of musical instruments throughout much of Iceland's history. Hallgrímur Pétursson wrote many Protestant hymns in the 17th century. Icelandic music was modernised in the 19th century when Magnús Stephensen brought pipe organs, which were followed by [harmoniums](/wiki/Pump_organ "Pump organ"). Other vital traditions of Icelandic music are epic, alliterative, and rhyming ballads called *rímur*. *Rímur* are epic tales, usually [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella"), which can be traced back to [skaldic](/wiki/Skald "Skald") poetry, using complex metaphors and elaborate rhyme schemes.[[272]](#cite_note-276) The best-known rímur poet of the 19th century was [Sigurður Breiðfjörð](/wiki/Sigur%C3%B0ur_Brei%C3%B0fj%C3%B6r%C3%B0 "Sigurður Breiðfjörð") (1798–1846). A modern revitalisation of the tradition began in 1929 with the formation of [Kvæðamannafélagið Iðunn](/w/index.php?title=Kv%C3%A6%C3%B0amannaf%C3%A9lagi%C3%B0_I%C3%B0unn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kvæðamannafélagið Iðunn (page does not exist)") [[is](https://is.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kv%C3%A6%C3%B0amannaf%C3%A9lagi%C3%B0_I%C3%B0unn "is:Kvæðamannafélagið Iðunn")].[[273]](#cite_note-277)[[274]](#cite_note-278)

Among Iceland's best-known classical composers are [Daníel Bjarnason](/wiki/Dan%C3%ADel_Bjarnason "Daníel Bjarnason") and [Anna S. Þorvaldsdóttir](/wiki/Anna_S._%C3%9Eorvaldsd%C3%B3ttir "Anna S. Þorvaldsdóttir"), who in 2012 received the [Nordic Council Music Prize](/wiki/Nordic_Council_Music_Prize "Nordic Council Music Prize") and in 2015 was chosen as the [New York Philharmonic](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic")'s Kravis Emerging Composer, an honor which includes a $50,000 cash prize and a commission to write a composition for the orchestra; she is the second recipient.[[275]](#cite_note-279)

The [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of Iceland is *[Lofsöngur](/wiki/Lofs%C3%B6ngur "Lofsöngur")*, written by [Matthías Jochumsson](/wiki/Matth%C3%ADas_Jochumsson "Matthías Jochumsson"), with music by [Sveinbjörn Sveinbjörnsson](/wiki/Sveinbj%C3%B6rn_Sveinbj%C3%B6rnsson "Sveinbjörn Sveinbjörnsson").[[276]](#cite_note-280)

### Media

See also: [Mass media in Iceland](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Iceland "Mass media in Iceland") and [Cinema of Iceland](/wiki/Cinema_of_Iceland "Cinema of Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Baltasar_Kormakur.jpg/250px-Baltasar_Kormakur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baltasar_Kormakur.jpg)

Icelandic director [Baltasar Kormákur](/wiki/Baltasar_Korm%C3%A1kur "Baltasar Kormákur"), best known for the films *[101 Reykjavík](/wiki/101_Reykjav%C3%ADk "101 Reykjavík")*, *[Jar City](/wiki/Jar_City_(film) "Jar City (film)")* and [*Contraband*](/wiki/Contraband_(2012_film) "Contraband (2012 film)"), and television series *[Trapped](/wiki/Trapped_(Icelandic_TV_series) "Trapped (Icelandic TV series)")*

Iceland's largest television stations are the state-run [Sjónvarpið](/wiki/Sj%C3%B3nvarpi%C3%B0 "Sjónvarpið") and the privately owned [Stöð 2](/wiki/St%C3%B6%C3%B0_2 "Stöð 2") and [SkjárEinn](/wiki/Skj%C3%A1rEinn "SkjárEinn").[[277]](#cite_note-281)[[278]](#cite_note-282) Smaller stations exist, many of them local. Radio is broadcast throughout the country including in some parts of the interior.[[279]](#cite_note-283)[[280]](#cite_note-284) The main radio stations are [Rás 1](/wiki/R%C3%A1s_1 "Rás 1"), [Rás 2](/wiki/R%C3%A1s_2 "Rás 2"), X-ið 977, [Bylgjan](/wiki/Bylgjan "Bylgjan") and [FM957](/wiki/FM_957_(Icelandic_Radio_Station) "FM 957 (Icelandic Radio Station)").[[281]](#cite_note-285) The daily newspapers are *[Morgunblaðið](/wiki/Morgunbla%C3%B0i%C3%B0 "Morgunblaðið")* and *[Fréttablaðið](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9ttabla%C3%B0i%C3%B0 "Fréttablaðið")*.[[282]](#cite_note-286) The most popular websites are the news sites [Vísir](/wiki/V%C3%ADsir "Vísir") and [Mbl.is](/wiki/Mbl.is "Mbl.is").[[283]](#cite_note-287)

Iceland is home to *[LazyTown](/wiki/LazyTown "LazyTown")* (Icelandic: *Latibær*), a children's educational musical comedy programme created by [Magnús Scheving](/wiki/Magn%C3%BAs_Scheving "Magnús Scheving"). It has become a very popular programme for children and adults and is shown in over 100 countries, including the Americas, the UK and Sweden.[[284]](#cite_note-288) The *LazyTown* studios are located in [Garðabær](/wiki/Gar%C3%B0ab%C3%A6r "Garðabær").[[285]](#cite_note-289)[[286]](#cite_note-290) The 2015 television crime series *[Trapped](/wiki/Trapped_(Icelandic_TV_series) "Trapped (Icelandic TV series)")* aired in the UK on [BBC4](/wiki/BBC4 "BBC4") in February and March 2016, to critical acclaim and according to [*The Guardian*](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") "the unlikeliest TV hit of the year".[[287]](#cite_note-guardian-291)

In 1992, the Icelandic film industry achieved its greatest recognition hitherto, when [Friðrik Þór Friðriksson](/wiki/Fri%C3%B0rik_%C3%9E%C3%B3r_Fri%C3%B0riksson "Friðrik Þór Friðriksson") was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best International Feature Film](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_International_Feature_Film "Academy Award for Best International Feature Film") for his *[Children of Nature](/wiki/Children_of_Nature "Children of Nature")*.[[288]](#cite_note-292) It features the story of an old man who is unable to continue running his farm. After being unwelcome in his daughter's and father-in-law's house in town, he is put in a home for the elderly. There, he meets an old girlfriend from his youth, and they both begin a journey through the wilds of Iceland to die together. This is the only Icelandic movie to have ever been nominated for an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award").[[289]](#cite_note-Oscars1992-293)

Singer-songwriter [Björk](/wiki/Bj%C3%B6rk "Björk") received international acclaim for her starring role in the Danish musical drama *[Dancer in the Dark](/wiki/Dancer_in_the_Dark "Dancer in the Dark")*, directed by [Lars von Trier](/wiki/Lars_von_Trier "Lars von Trier"), in which she plays Selma Ježková, a factory worker who struggles to pay for her son's eye operation. The film premiered at the [2000 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/2000_Cannes_Film_Festival "2000 Cannes Film Festival"), where she won the [Best Actress Award](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival_Award_for_Best_Actress "Cannes Film Festival Award for Best Actress"). The movie also led Björk to nominations for [Best Original Song](/wiki/Best_Original_Song "Best Original Song") at the [73rd Academy Awards](/wiki/73rd_Academy_Awards "73rd Academy Awards"), with the song *[I've Seen It All](/wiki/I%27ve_Seen_It_All "I've Seen It All")* and for a [Golden Globe Award for Best Actress in a Motion Picture – Drama](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_in_a_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress in a Motion Picture – Drama").[[290]](#cite_note-294) Guðrún S. Gísladóttir, who is Icelandic, played one of the major roles in Russian filmmaker [Andrei Tarkovsky](/wiki/Andrei_Tarkovsky "Andrei Tarkovsky")'s film *[The Sacrifice](/wiki/The_Sacrifice_(1986_film) "The Sacrifice (1986 film)")* (1986).[[291]](#cite_note-295) [Anita Briem](/wiki/Anita_Briem "Anita Briem"), known for her performance in [Showtime](/wiki/Showtime_(TV_channel) "Showtime (TV channel)")'s *[The Tudors](/wiki/The_Tudors "The Tudors")*,[[292]](#cite_note-296) is also Icelandic. Briem starred in the film *[Journey to the Center of the Earth](/wiki/Journey_to_the_Center_of_the_Earth_(2008_theatrical_film) "Journey to the Center of the Earth (2008 theatrical film)")* (2008), which shot in Iceland. The [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") movie *[Die Another Day](/wiki/Die_Another_Day "Die Another Day")* (2002) is set for a large part in Iceland.[[293]](#cite_note-297)[[294]](#cite_note-298) [Christopher Nolan](/wiki/Christopher_Nolan "Christopher Nolan")'s film *[Interstellar](/wiki/Interstellar_(film) "Interstellar (film)")* (2014) was also filmed in Iceland for some of its scenes,[[295]](#cite_note-299) as was [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott")'s *[Prometheus](/wiki/Prometheus_(2012_film) "Prometheus (2012 film)")* (2012).[[296]](#cite_note-300)

On 17 June 2010, the parliament passed the [Icelandic Modern Media Initiative](/wiki/Icelandic_Modern_Media_Initiative "Icelandic Modern Media Initiative"), proposing greater protection of free speech rights and the identity of journalists and whistle-blowers—the strongest journalist protection law in the world.[[297]](#cite_note-301) According to a 2024 report by [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House"), Iceland is one of the highest-ranked countries in press freedom.[[298]](#cite_note-302)

[CCP Games](/wiki/CCP_Games "CCP Games"), developers of the critically acclaimed *[EVE Online](/wiki/Eve_Online "Eve Online")* and *[Dust 514](/wiki/Dust_514 "Dust 514")*, are headquartered in Reykjavík.[[299]](#cite_note-303) CCP Games hosts the third-most populated [MMO](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_game "Massively multiplayer online game") in the world, which also has the largest total game area for an online game, according to *Guinness World Records*.[[300]](#cite_note-304)

Iceland has a highly developed [internet culture](/wiki/Cyberculture "Cyberculture"), with around 99.8% of the population having internet access, the highest proportion in the world.[[301]](#cite_note-305) Iceland ranked 16th in the World Economic Forum's 2016 [Network Readiness Index](/wiki/Network_Readiness_Index "Network Readiness Index"), which measures a country's ability to competitively exploit communications technology.[[302]](#cite_note-306) The [United Nations International Telecommunication Union](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union") ranked the country first in its development of information and communications technology in 2017, having moved up one place from 2016.[[303]](#cite_note-307) In February 2013 the country (ministry of the interior) was researching possible methods to protect children in regards to Internet pornography, claiming that pornography online is a threat to children as it supports child slavery and abuse. Strong voices within the community expressed concerns with this, stating that it is impossible to block access to pornography without compromising freedom of speech.[[304]](#cite_note-308)[[305]](#cite_note-309)[[306]](#cite_note-310)

### Cuisine

Main articles: [Icelandic cuisine](/wiki/Icelandic_cuisine "Icelandic cuisine") and [Þorramatur](/wiki/%C3%9Eorramatur "Þorramatur")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Thorramatur.jpg/250px-Thorramatur.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thorramatur.jpg)

A typical [Þorramatur](/wiki/%C3%9Eorramatur "Þorramatur") assortment

Much of Iceland's cuisine is based on fish, [lamb](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton"), and [dairy products](/wiki/Dairy_product "Dairy product"), with little to no use of herbs or spices. Due to the island's climate, fruits and vegetables are not generally a component of traditional dishes, although the use of greenhouses has made them more common in contemporary food. [Þorramatur](/wiki/%C3%9Eorramatur "Þorramatur") is a selection of traditional cuisine consisting of many dishes and is usually consumed around the month of [Þorri](/wiki/Month#Germanic_calendar "Month"), which begins on the first Friday after 19 January. Traditional dishes also include [skyr](/wiki/Skyr "Skyr") (a yogurt-like cheese), [hákarl](/wiki/H%C3%A1karl "Hákarl") (cured shark), cured ram, singed sheep heads, and [black pudding](/wiki/Black_pudding "Black pudding"), Flatkaka (flatbread), dried fish, and dark rye bread traditionally baked in the ground in geothermal areas.[[307]](#cite_note-Icelandic_delicacies-311) [Puffin](/wiki/Puffin "Puffin") is considered a local delicacy which is often prepared through broiling.

Breakfast usually consists of pancakes, cereal, fruit, and coffee, while lunch may take the form of a [smörgåsbord](/wiki/Sm%C3%B6rg%C3%A5sbord "Smörgåsbord"). The main meal of the day for most Icelanders is dinner, which usually involves fish or lamb as the main course. Seafood is central to most Icelandic cooking, particularly [cod](/wiki/Cod "Cod") and [haddock](/wiki/Haddock "Haddock") but also [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon"), [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring"), and [halibut](/wiki/Halibut "Halibut"). It is often prepared in a wide variety of ways, either smoked, pickled, boiled, or dried. Lamb is by far the most common meat, and it tends to be either smoke-cured (known as *[hangikjöt](/wiki/Hangikj%C3%B6t "Hangikjöt")*) or salt-preserved (*saltkjöt*). Many older dishes make use of every part of the sheep, such as *[slátur](/wiki/Sl%C3%A1tur "Slátur")*, which consists of [offal](/wiki/Offal "Offal") (internal organs and entrails) minced together with blood and served in the sheep's stomach. Additionally, boiled or mashed potatoes, pickled cabbage, green beans, and rye bread are prevalent side dishes.[[308]](#cite_note-312)[[309]](#cite_note-313)

Coffee is a popular beverage in Iceland, with the country being third placed by per capita consumption worldwide in 2016,[[310]](#cite_note-314) and is drunk at breakfast, after meals, and with a light snack in mid-afternoon. [Coca-Cola](/wiki/Coca-Cola "Coca-Cola") is also widely consumed, to the extent that the country is said to have one of the highest per capita consumption rates in the world.[[311]](#cite_note-315)

Iceland's signature alcoholic beverage is *[brennivín](/wiki/Brenniv%C3%ADn "Brennivín")* (literally "burnt [i.e., distilled] wine"), which is similar in flavouring to the [akvavit](/wiki/Akvavit "Akvavit") variant of Scandinavian [brännvin](/wiki/Br%C3%A4nnvin "Brännvin"). It is a type of [schnapps](/wiki/Schnapps "Schnapps") made from distilled potatoes and flavoured with either [caraway](/wiki/Caraway "Caraway") seeds or [angelica](/wiki/Angelica "Angelica"). Its potency has earned it the nickname *svarti dauði* ("Black Death"). Modern distilleries on Iceland produce [vodka](/wiki/Vodka "Vodka") ([Reyka](/wiki/Reyka "Reyka")), [gin](/wiki/Gin "Gin") (Ísafold), [moss](/wiki/Moss "Moss") schnapps (Fjallagrasa), and a [birch](/wiki/Birch "Birch")-flavored schnapps and [liqueur](/wiki/Liqueur "Liqueur") (Foss Distillery's Birkir and Björk). Martin Miller blends Icelandic water with its England-distilled gin on the island. Strong beer was [banned until 1989](/wiki/Prohibition_in_Iceland "Prohibition in Iceland"), so *bjórlíki*, a mixture of legal, low-alcohol [pilsner](/wiki/Pilsner "Pilsner") beer and vodka, became popular. Several strong beers are now made by Icelandic breweries.

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Iceland](/wiki/Sport_in_Iceland "Sport in Iceland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Iceland%27s_Olympic_Handball_Team%2C_Beijing_2008.jpg/250px-Iceland%27s_Olympic_Handball_Team%2C_Beijing_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iceland%27s_Olympic_Handball_Team,_Beijing_2008.jpg)

The [Iceland men's national handball team](/wiki/Iceland_men%27s_national_handball_team "Iceland men's national handball team") (pictured) [won the silver medal](/wiki/Handball_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Handball at the 2008 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") at the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"). [Handball](/wiki/Team_handball "Team handball") is considered to be Iceland's national sport.[[312]](#cite_note-handball-316)

Sport is an important part of Icelandic culture, as the population is generally quite active.[[313]](#cite_note-317) The main traditional sport in Iceland is *[Glíma](/wiki/Gl%C3%ADma "Glíma")*, a form of [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling") thought to have originated in medieval times.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/FWC_2018_-_Group_D_-_ARG_v_ISL_-_Photo_195.jpg/250px-FWC_2018_-_Group_D_-_ARG_v_ISL_-_Photo_195.jpg)](/wiki/File:FWC_2018_-_Group_D_-_ARG_v_ISL_-_Photo_195.jpg)

Iceland fans at the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup") at [Spartak Stadium](/wiki/Spartak_Stadium_(Moscow) "Spartak Stadium (Moscow)") in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow")

Popular sports include [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field"), [handball](/wiki/Team_handball "Team handball"), and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"). Handball is often referred to as the national sport.[[312]](#cite_note-handball-316) The [Icelandic national football team](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team") qualified for the [2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") [UEFA European football championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Championship "UEFA European Championship") for the first time. They recorded a draw against later winners [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_national_football_team "Portuguese national football team") in the group stage, and defeated [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") 2–1 in the round of 16, with goals from [Ragnar Sigurðsson](/wiki/Ragnar_Sigur%C3%B0sson "Ragnar Sigurðsson") and [Kolbeinn Sigþórsson](/wiki/Kolbeinn_Sig%C3%BE%C3%B3rsson "Kolbeinn Sigþórsson"). They then lost to hosts and later finalists [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team") in the quarter-finals.[[314]](#cite_note-Guardian_2017-08-03-318) Afterwards, Iceland made its debut at the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup"). For both the European and the world championships, Iceland was the smallest nation in terms of population to qualify at the time.

Iceland is also the smallest country to ever qualify for [Eurobasket](/wiki/Eurobasket "Eurobasket"), having done so in both 2015 and 2017. However, they have not managed to win a single game in the European Basketball final stages.

Iceland has excellent conditions for [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing"), [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing"), [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding"), [ice climbing](/wiki/Ice_climbing "Ice climbing") and [rock climbing](/wiki/Rock_climbing "Rock climbing"), although [mountain climbing](/wiki/Mountain_climbing "Mountain climbing") and [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") are preferred by the general public. Iceland is also a world-class destination for alpine ski touring and [Telemark skiing](/wiki/Telemark_skiing "Telemark skiing"), with the Troll Peninsula in Northern Iceland being the main centre of activity. Although the country's environment is generally ill-suited for golf, nevertheless there are many golf courses throughout the island and Iceland has a greater percentage of the population playing golf than Scotland, with over 17,000 registered golfers out of a population of approximately 300,000.[[315]](#cite_note-319) Iceland hosts an annual international golf tournament known as the Arctic Open played through the night during the summer solstice at [Akureyri Golf Club](/wiki/Akureyri_Golf_Club "Akureyri Golf Club").[[316]](#cite_note-320)[[317]](#cite_note-w111-321) Iceland has won the second most [World's Strongest Man](/wiki/World%27s_Strongest_Man "World's Strongest Man") competitions of any country with nine titles, including four each by [Magnús Ver Magnússon](/wiki/Magn%C3%BAs_Ver_Magn%C3%BAsson "Magnús Ver Magnússon") and [Jón Páll Sigmarsson](/wiki/J%C3%B3n_P%C3%A1ll_Sigmarsson "Jón Páll Sigmarsson"), and most recently by [Hafþór Júlíus Björnsson](/wiki/Haf%C3%BE%C3%B3r_J%C3%BAl%C3%ADus_Bj%C3%B6rnsson "Hafþór Júlíus Björnsson") in 2018.

Iceland is also one of the leading countries in [ocean rowing](/wiki/Ocean_rowing "Ocean rowing"). Icelandic explorer and endurance athlete [Fiann Paul](/wiki/Fiann_Paul "Fiann Paul") holds the highest number of performance-based *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* within a single athletic discipline. As of 2020, he is the first and only person to achieve the [Ocean Explorers Grand Slam](/wiki/Ocean_Explorers_Grand_Slam "Ocean Explorers Grand Slam") (performing open-water crossings on each of the five oceans using human-powered vessels) and has claimed overall speed *Guinness World Records* for the fastest rowing of all four oceans (Atlantic, Indian, Pacific, and the Arctic) in a [human-powered row boat](/wiki/Human-powered_watercraft "Human-powered watercraft"). He had achieved 41 records, including 33 performance based *Guinness World Records* by 2020.[[318]](#cite_note-322)[[319]](#cite_note-323)[[320]](#cite_note-324)[[321]](#cite_note-325)[[322]](#cite_note-326)

Swimming is popular in Iceland. Geothermally heated outdoor pools are widespread, and swimming courses are a mandatory part of the national curriculum.[[317]](#cite_note-w111-321) [Horseback riding](/wiki/Horseback_riding "Horseback riding"), which was historically the most prevalent form of transportation on the island, remains a common pursuit for many Icelanders. The oldest sports association in Iceland is the Reykjavík Shooting Association, founded in 1867. Rifle shooting became very popular in the 19th century with the encouragement of politicians and nationalists who pushed for Icelandic independence. Even now, it remains a significant pastime.[[323]](#cite_note-327)

Iceland has produced many chess masters and hosted the historic [World Chess Championship 1972](/wiki/World_Chess_Championship_1972 "World Chess Championship 1972") in Reykjavík during the height of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Iceland&action=edit), there have been nine Icelandic chess grandmasters, a considerable number related to the small size of the population of the country.[[324]](#cite_note-328) [Bridge](/wiki/Bridge_(card_game) "Bridge (card game)") is also popular, with Iceland participating in several international tournaments. Iceland won the world bridge championship (the [Bermuda Bowl](/wiki/Bermuda_Bowl "Bermuda Bowl")) in [Yokohama, Japan](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama") in 1991 and came in second place (with Sweden) in [Hamilton, Bermuda](/wiki/Hamilton,_Bermuda "Hamilton, Bermuda"), in 1950.[[325]](#cite_note-329)
